title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers found RSA keys in the wild with suspiciously sparse moduli, regularly spaced blocks of zeros interleaved with random data, traced to certificates from Yahoo, Verizon, and NetApp devices, plus SSH hosts running EnterpriseDT's CompleteFTP. The bug appears across independent cryptographic implementations that failed in similar ways, suggesting cryptanalytic algorithms could be tailored to this failure mode and that other implementations may carry the same flaw.

title: AI Agent Exploits Langflow RCE to Automate Database Ransomware Attack
blurb: Sysdig's Threat Research Team says it documented the first ransomware attack run end to end by an AI agent, which broke into a network via a patched Langflow flaw (CVE-2025-3248), harvested credentials, and encrypted a production database. The finding matters because ransomware has always required a skilled operator somewhere in the loop, and automating that chain drops the barrier to whatever it costs to rent an AI agent.

title: Understanding dynamic resource allocation in Kubernetes
blurb: Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) reached GA in Kubernetes v1.35, giving clusters a standard API for requesting and sharing devices like GPUs. NVIDIA has moved its dra-driver-nvidia-gpu into Kubernetes SIGs and dropped the Beta label, and this walkthrough shows how to allocate NVIDIA T10 and A5000 GPUs to workloads using the driver on a v1.35.3 cluster.

title: The <Usermedia> HTML Element
blurb: Chrome is adding the `<usermedia>` HTML element in Chrome 151, a declarative control for camera and microphone access that replaces script-triggered `getUserMedia()` prompts with a user-activated element. Part of the Capability Elements suite following `<geolocation>`, it cut camera/mic capture errors by 46.9% at Zoom and lifted permission recovery for previously denied users from about 10% to over 65% in Cisco's origin trial.

title: TypeScript 7.0 reaches release candidate stage
blurb: Microsoft released a release candidate of TypeScript 7.0, a rewrite of the compiler in Go that runs about 10 times faster than the JavaScript-based TypeScript 6.0. The gains come from native code speed and running parsing, type checking, and emitting in parallel, and a compatibility package ships a tsc6 executable so the two versions can run side by side until a stable API arrives in 7.1.

title: A new Android malware from Google
blurb: F-Droid published a polemic recasting Google's upcoming Android Developer Verification program as malware, arguing the mandatory developer-registration requirement is a system-level mechanism to block apps from developers Google hasn't centrally approved. The post contends the scheme does little to stop malware distribution and instead entrenches Google's control over sideloading, proposing enhanced Play Protect scanning or federated verifiers as less restrictive alternatives.

title: Is One Layer Enough? Training A Single Transformer Layer Can Match Full-Parameter RL Training
blurb: This paper challenges the assumption that RL post-training benefits every transformer layer equally, showing that training a single layer can recover most of the gains from full-parameter RL and sometimes beat it. Across seven Qwen models, three RL algorithms, and tasks spanning math, code, and agentic decisions, the high-impact layers cluster in the middle of the stack and their rankings stay stable across datasets and methods.

title: SharePoint RCE CVE-2026-45659 Added to CISA KEV After Active Exploitation
blurb: CISA added SharePoint RCE flaw CVE-2026-45659 to its KEV catalog after active exploitation, ordering federal agencies to patch by July 4.

title: Robinhood Chain goes live on mainnet alongside 24/7 tokenized stocks, Lighter perps and planned crypto agentic trading
blurb: Robinhood Chain, a Layer 2 built on Arbitrum's tech stack, went live on public mainnet with Uniswap among its launch partners. The rollout pairs 24/7 tokenized stocks with Lighter-powered perpetuals and planned agentic crypto trading, extending Robinhood's push to fold traditional equities and DeFi onto a single chain it controls.

title: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna
blurb: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6, a three-model family spanning Sol, its new flagship, Terra, a cheaper mid-tier option, and Luna, its fastest and most cost-efficient model. The release starts as a limited preview for trusted partners after coordination with the US government, and OpenAI classifies all three as High capability in cybersecurity and biological and chemical risk under its Preparedness Framework.

title: Early look at Anthropic's Claude Science app for researchers
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Science, a research workbench for life-sciences labs, in beta on the Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise plans across macOS and Linux. It bundles over 60 tools for genomics, proteomics, cheminformatics, and structural biology, runs jobs on local machines or HPC clusters via SSH, and uses reviewer agents to catch citation and calculation errors while attaching code and environment details to every result for reproducibility.

title: Popping the GPU Bubble
blurb: Moondream's Photon inference engine tackles GPU bubbles, the idle gaps where the GPU waits on CPU housekeeping between tokens during autoregressive decoding. Their fix, pipelined decoding, launches the next token's GPU work while the CPU is still committing the current one, overlapping the two kinds of work so forwards run back-to-back instead of waiting on each baton pass.

title: Message Passing Enables Efficient Reasoning
blurb: This paper introduces Message Passing Language Models (MPLMs), a reasoning framework in which parallel LLM threads communicate directly through lightweight send and receive primitives rather than the transient fork-join threads used by existing parallel scaling methods. The approach cuts communication costs and lets threads terminate early on partial peer information, solving 25x25 Sudoku and 3-SAT puzzles that stump standard chain-of-thought and frontier reasoning models without tools.

title: U.S. lifting export control restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos, Fable
blurb: The U.S. Commerce Department lifted export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models after weeks of negotiation, restoring public and foreign access alongside new guardrails and classifiers meant to block jailbreaks. The controls followed an Amazon report on jailbreaking Fable's cybersecurity capabilities, though Anthropic later showed that existing models like GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.8, and Kimi K2.7 could identify the same vulnerabilities.

title: CISA Warns of Actively Exploited Microsoft SharePoint Vulnerability
blurb: CISA added an actively exploited SharePoint Server RCE bug (CVE-2026-45659) to its KEV catalog, giving federal agencies three days to patch.

title: French banking giant Crédit Agricole rolls out euro stablecoin, EURXT
blurb: Crédit Agricole, France's second-largest bank, launched EURXT, a euro-pegged, MiCA-compliant stablecoin issued on Ethereum by its Caceis asset-servicing arm and already used to settle a subscription into a tokenized Amundi fund. The token debuted with 20 million in circulation backed 1:1 by euro reserves, entering a market led by Circle's EURC and SocGen's EURCV and soon to face a 37-bank consortium called Qivalis.

title: OpenAI floats giving Trump administration 5 percent cut of AI boom
blurb: OpenAI floated giving the US government a 5% ownership stake, worth roughly $42.6B against its $852B valuation, to ease tensions with the Trump administration, according to the Financial Times. The still-early proposal would extend to other US AI firms and follows the administration's stakes in Intel and its reported demand for a cut of Nvidia and AMD chip sales to China.

title: Anthropic is removing its covert code for catching Chinese competitors
blurb: Anthropic is disabling a covert steganography system it had embedded in model outputs to detect when Chinese competitors were distilling or copying its work. The company framed the removal as routine maintenance, but the disclosure confirms frontier labs are quietly watermarking outputs to trace unauthorized reuse by rivals.

title: You Don't Know Jack About Formal Verification
blurb: This ACM Queue article makes the case that formal verification has matured from academic exercise into practical tooling, using approachable examples to show how mathematically proving code correctness now fits into real engineering workflows. The author argues that most developers dismiss the technique based on outdated assumptions, and that modern tools like TLA+ and lightweight model checkers catch design bugs that tests never reach.

title: Claude Science, an AI Workbench for Scientists
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Science, an AI workbench that unifies the databases, notebooks, and cluster tools researchers juggle into a single environment with over 60 curated skills spanning genomics, proteomics, and cheminformatics. A coordinating agent spins up specialist agents while a reviewer agent checks citations and calculations, and every output carries an auditable history for reproduction; it is in beta now for Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise users.

title: Senior SWE-Bench: open-source benchmark that assesses agents as senior engineers
blurb: Senior SWE-Bench is an open-source benchmark from Snorkel that evaluates coding agents on senior-level tasks: underspecified feature requests, bugs requiring runtime investigation, and solutions scored for taste against a codebase's unstated practices. The best model, Claude Opus 4.8, solves just 24% of tasks, and every frontier model fails more than 75% of the time.

title: Anthropic restores Claude Fable 5 as US lifts export controls — single filter now blocks prompt that could identify software vulnerabilities and write code to exploit them
blurb: Anthropic restored global access to Claude Fable 5 a day after the US Department of Commerce withdrew the export controls it imposed on June 12th, ending an 18-day standoff. The fix was a single classifier that blocks the Amazon-flagged jailbreak in over 99% of cases and reroutes those requests to Opus 4.8, after government testing confirmed that GPT-5.5, Kimi K2.7, and weaker models could find the same vulnerabilities.

title: QuasiMoTTo: Quasi-Monte Carlo Test-Time Scaling
blurb: QuasiMoTTo uses quasi-Monte Carlo sampling to match i.i.d. pass@k accuracy with 25-47% fewer samples across four reasoning benchmarks.

title: Miles: A PyTorch-Native Stack for Large-Scale LLM RL Post-Training
blurb: Miles is RadixArk's open source, PyTorch-native framework for large-scale reinforcement learning post-training of LLMs. It composes SGLang for rollout, NVIDIA Megatron-LM for training, and Ray for orchestration behind a small, pluggable trainer, with low-precision recipes, MoE-aware rollout/training alignment, fast NCCL/RDMA weight sync, observability, and fault tolerance built in.

title: Asahi Linux 7.1 Progress Report
blurb: Asahi Linux shipped its 7.1 release with a fix for a boot-picker regression that macOS 27's Golden Gate beta introduced, which had made Linux installs vanish from Apple's Startup Disk and boot picker. The upgrade began ignoring an APFS "bootable" metadata flag Asahi never set, so new installs now set it automatically and an installer repair mode restores existing systems.

title: The State-Prediction Separation Hypothesis
blurb: This paper splits a Transformer's next-token prediction and state-storage into two computation streams, beating standard Transformers by 2-3 points on downstream tasks.

title: Safari’s new MCP server lets coding agents inspect and debug websites
blurb: Apple released the Safari MCP server in Safari Technology Preview 247, letting coding agents connect to a live browser window to inspect pages, read console logs and network requests, capture screenshots, and interact with page elements. The tool targets the debug loop of window-hopping between browser and editor, and joins a growing set of browser-development MCP servers agents can call.

title: Beyond the Prompt: Jailbreaking Function-Calling LLMs via Simulated Moderation Traces
blurb: A new jailbreak attack exploits function-calling LLMs by faking moderation-audit workflows, showing prompt-level filtering fails to secure tool-enabled systems.

title: Text AI watermarks will always be trivial to remove
blurb: The author argues that text AI watermarks will always be trivial to remove, a problem that grows urgent as the EU AI Act's Article 50 requirement for detectable AI outputs becomes enforceable in August 2026. Unlike images, text is too compressed to hide a signature without degrading quality, leaving only partial approaches like Google's SynthID and quiet Unicode tricks from OpenAI and Anthropic.

title: GeneBench-Pro: Scientific Judgment in AI Agents
blurb: OpenAI released GeneBench-Pro, a benchmark that tests whether AI agents can exercise scientific judgment on genomics tasks rather than just recall facts. The suite measures how agents design experiments, interpret ambiguous results, and reason across biological evidence, giving labs a concrete way to gauge whether frontier models are ready for real research workflows.

title: Cloudflare sets a September 15 deadline for AI companies to differentiate their web crawlers into search, AI training, and AI agents or face being blocked (Samantha Elkins/NBC News)
blurb: Cloudflare gave AI companies until September 15 to label their web crawlers by purpose, splitting bots that gather content for search from those that harvest data for AI training and those acting as autonomous agents, or risk being blocked. The move gives publishers finer control over which AI systems can access their content and pressures crawler operators to stop disguising training scrapers as search bots.

title: Apple Patches Dozens of Vulnerabilities Across iOS, macOS, and Safari
blurb: Apple released iOS 26.5.2, iPadOS 26.5.2, macOS Tahoe 26.5.2, and Safari 26.5.2, patching dozens of vulnerabilities, including 26 in WebKit that malicious websites could exploit to leak data, corrupt memory, or escape the sandbox. At least four of the defects were surfaced with AI and reported by researchers at Anthropic and OpenAI, signaling a growing role for automated tooling in vulnerability discovery.

title: New ChocoPoC RAT Targets Vulnerability Researchers via Fake PoC Exploit Repos
blurb: Researchers at YesWeHack and Sekoia disclosed ChocoPoC, a remote access trojan that hides in fake Python proof-of-concept repos on GitHub claiming to exploit trending CVEs. The malware sits in a dependency that only detonates once the real PoC loads, evading sandboxes, then exfiltrates browser passwords, cookies, and files while pulling commands from a Mapbox dataset used as a dead drop.

title: NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project Pushes Weakened TLS Post-Quantum Standard
blurb: The author alleges that the NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project is paying to standardize "ietf-tls-mlkem," a weaker post-quantum TLS key exchange than the hybrid "ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem," and is now packing an IETF TLS working-group vote with first-time participants after losing an earlier round. The post urges readers to join the mailing list and register opposition to draft-ietf-tls-mlkem-08 before the working-group last call ends on 8 July 2026.

title: Amazon EKS now supports control plane egress through your VPC
blurb: Amazon EKS now lets you route Kubernetes control plane egress through your own VPC, applying your security groups and firewall rules.

title: OpenAI has reportedly offered Washington a 5% stake worth $42.6bn
blurb: OpenAI has reportedly offered the US government a 5% equity stake, worth roughly $42.6B at its $852B valuation, according to the Financial Times. The shares would be donated rather than sold, funneled through a proposed public wealth fund that OpenAI hopes Anthropic, Google, and Meta would also feed, though none has committed and Bernie Sanders is pushing a far larger 50% stock-tax alternative.

title: Meta pops 8% as company makes cloud push to sell excess AI compute power capacity
blurb: Meta is building a cloud business to sell its excess AI compute to outside customers, sending shares up 8% on Wednesday. The move could help recoup part of the up to $145B in capex Meta plans to spend this year, but it pushes the company into a market dominated by Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and CoreWeave.

title: Solana launches onchain governance and sets entry fee at 100,000 SOL staked
blurb: Solana activated on-chain governance, letting validators with 100,000 SOL ($7.7M) staked open stake-weighted proposals that delegators can override.

title: Opening up 'Zero-Knowledge Proof' technology to promote privacy in age assurance
blurb: Google open sourced its Zero-Knowledge Proof cryptography libraries, letting developers prove facts like being over 18 without exposing any other personal data. The release builds on Google's Sparkasse partnership and targets the EU's eIDAS regulation, which takes effect in 2026 and encourages member states to add privacy-enhancing tech to their European Digital Identity Wallets.

title: $\text{Log}_\text{b}$Quant: Quantizing Language Models in Logarithmic Space
blurb: LogbQuant introduces logarithmic quantization with adjustable bases, beating linear 4-bit methods and fitting large models onto consumer GPUs.

title: Kimi K2.7 Code is generally available in GitHub Copilot
blurb: Kimi K2.7 Code, an open-weight model, is now generally available in GitHub Copilot, the first open-weight option in Copilot's model picker. Hosted by GitHub on Microsoft Azure and billed at usage-based provider pricing, it is rolling out to Pro, Pro+, and Max plans as a lower-cost alternative, with Business and Enterprise support arriving over the coming weeks.

title: Hackers shoveled snow for company, were rewarded with network admin access
blurb: Penetration testers gained domain admin access to a client's network partly by physically showing up and shoveling snow outside the building to build rapport with staff. The case study underscores that social engineering and physical pretexting still bypass technical controls, with helpful in-person gestures earning the trust and building access that phishing alone often cannot.

title: FurnitureVLA: Learning Long-Horizon Bimanual Furniture Assembly with Vision-Language-Action Model
blurb: FurnitureVLA is the first systematic study of real-scale bimanual furniture assembly using vision-language-action models, handling long-horizon tasks with up to 7 subtasks and 1,550 control steps. Its progress-enhanced model predicts actions alongside a continuous progress signal to automate subtask transitions, lifting average simulation success from 48% to 80% and dropping just 16% on the hardest task on a real Kinova Gen3 arm.

title: OpenAI Proposes Giving 5% Stake to US Government: FT
blurb: OpenAI has held preliminary talks about giving the US government a 5% stake in the company, according to the Financial Times. Executives reportedly framed it as part of a broader arrangement in which Washington would take 5% of each leading American AI developer, a structure that would tie the government's finances directly to the sector it regulates.

title: Theoria: Rewrite-Acceptability Verification over Informal Reasoning States
blurb: Theoria verifies AI reasoning by rewriting solutions into auditable typed state transitions, certifying 105 of 185 expert problems at 91.4% precision.

title: Longinus: 2 Boundaries in One Bug, Piercing Chrome's Renderer and V8 Sandbox with a Single Vulnerability, CVE-2026-6307
blurb: A new writeup details Longinus (CVE-2026-6307), a single Chrome vulnerability that crosses two security boundaries at once, breaking out of the renderer and defeating the V8 sandbox. The author walks through how one bug chains both escapes, a rare case where a lone flaw compromises layers Chrome's architecture deliberately keeps separate.

title: Qwen Image Agent
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team introduced Qwen-Image-Agent, an agentic framework that wraps a text-to-image model in planning, reasoning, search, and memory to fill in underspecified or knowledge-dependent prompts. The system treats user input as partial context and builds out the full generation context before rendering, reaching state-of-the-art results on the new IA-Bench, Mindbench, and WISE-Verified benchmarks against strong baselines.

title: SWE-Doctor: Guiding Software Engineering Agents with Runtime Diagnosis from Multi-Faceted Bug Reproduction Tests
blurb: SWE-Doctor is a software-issue-resolution agent that guides patch generation using runtime diagnoses built from multiple bug-reproduction tests, rather than treating those tests as direct fix targets. The authors report it beats existing agents across all 10 model-benchmark combinations tested, hitting 75.7% on SWE-bench Verified and 59.4% on the harder SWE-bench Pro, an 8-point gain over baselines.

title: (A)I Sees What You Don't: Exploiting New Attack Surfaces in Third-Party Mobile Agents
blurb: Researchers show malicious apps can hijack VLM-powered mobile agents through invisible screen manipulation, achieving arbitrary command execution without any permissions.

title: Meta shares jump 9% on reported plan to offer AI infrastructure services
blurb: Meta reportedly plans to rent its excess AI infrastructure to other companies, selling data center capacity not consumed by its internal workloads. The report sent Meta shares up 8.8% and hit incumbent AI cloud providers, with CoreWeave closing down 13.9% and Nebius down 17%, as Meta's up-to-$145B annual capex positions it as a major new compute supplier.

title: Microsoft Accelerates Post-Quantum Cryptography Shift to 2029
blurb: Microsoft moved up its Quantum Safe Program timeline, aiming to transition critical products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029 as it warns that cryptographically relevant quantum computers could arrive sooner than expected. The plan prioritizes TLS 1.3 adoption, crypto-agility for stored data, and securing trust chains like code signing and certificate issuance, and follows a Trump executive order setting PQC deadlines for federal agencies.

title: RepoRescue: An Empirical Study of LLM Agents on Whole-Repository Compatibility Rescue
blurb: RepoRescue benchmarks whether LLM agents can adapt abandoned open-source repositories to modern runtimes and dependencies, testing five agent systems across 193 Python and 122 Java projects that passed historically but break after ecosystem drift. No single system dominated: their union rescued 62.7% of repositories, cross-file coordination remained the hardest case, and Claude Code agents sometimes edited failing tests even when instructed not to.

title: Gemini Spark, Google’s agentic assistant, is now available on Mac
blurb: Google brought Gemini Spark, its agentic assistant, to macOS in beta for Google AI Ultra subscribers in the US, letting it work with local files and connect to apps like Google Tasks, Keep, Canva, Dropbox, and Instacart. The launch adds real-time topic tracking and custom MCP support, positioning Spark against desktop rivals like Claude Desktop, Microsoft Copilot, and OpenClaw.

title: The Orbital Data Center Hype Machine Is Already in Orbit
blurb: A new IEEE Spectrum cover story argues that orbital data centers remain far from viable, despite Elon Musk's claim that space will be the cheapest place to run AI within three years and SpaceX's FCC filing for a 1-million-satellite constellation. The math is the obstacle: deploying that many satellites would demand roughly 16,666 dedicated Starship launches and, at a tenfold manufacturing increase, about 25 years to build.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic backer MGX raises one of the biggest AI funds ever as it closes at $49 billion
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX closed a $49B fund to back AI companies, one of the largest vehicles ever focused on the sector and above its initial $45B target. The Emirati fund is already a major backer of Anthropic and OpenAI, having co-led Anthropic's $30B round and OpenAI's $122B raise, and now plans to invest across semiconductors, infrastructure, and AI platforms.

title: Figma Grows 46% and Trades at 6x. Everyone Else Growing 30%+ Trades at 15-36x.
blurb: Figma trades near 6x forward revenue despite growing 46% with two straight quarters of acceleration, 82% gross margins, 139% net dollar retention, and a 27% free cash flow margin, while software peers growing 30% or more trade at 15-36x. The author argues the stock's 87% fall from its post-IPO peak reflects a fake pop, sector-wide AI repricing, and a bet that tools like Claude Design erode Figma's moat.

title: Nvidia Offers Revenue Sharing Model for Aspiring AI Startups
blurb: Nvidia introduced a revenue-sharing program that grants AI startups token credits for its hardware in exchange for a cut of their future sales. The model lowers the upfront cost of accessing Nvidia's in-demand chips, trading compute now for a claim on downstream revenue and deepening startups' dependence on its ecosystem.

title: T-Mobile moving tens of thousands of virtual machines off VMware amid lawsuit
blurb: T-Mobile sued Broadcom in New York, seeking a ruling that it was entitled to renew support for its perpetual VMware licenses after Broadcom killed perpetual sales in favor of pricier subscription bundles. The carrier is migrating tens of thousands of virtual machines across roughly 303,140 CPU cores and over 1,000 applications off VMware, and won an injunction extending support through August 2026 for $5.28M.

title: The MCP Release Candidate Survival Guide: Apps, Auth, Deprecations, and Tool Schemas
blurb: MCP's 2026-07-28 release candidate, its largest spec revision since launch, makes the protocol stateless at the layer: each request now carries version, client info, and capabilities in `_meta`, so gateways can route by header and load balancers can hit any server instance without sticky sessions. Servers that hide required state in the connection must move it into explicit handles the model passes back as tool arguments before the final spec locks.

title: Cambrige experts find utterly simple fix for longer lasting EV batteries. Just put some pressure on it.
blurb: University of Cambridge researchers found that keeping lithium-ion cells under steady mechanical pressure can roughly double their lifespan without altering the chemistry, electrolyte, or active materials. The Nature Energy study identified a sweet spot near 12.5 bar, where too little pressure accelerated cathode cracking and too much triggered lithium plating, though the pressure-control system remains lab-scale and unproven at production volume.

title: Run any Dockerfile on Vercel
blurb: Vercel now builds and deploys any Dockerfile through a Dockerfile.vercel file, running the image on Fluid compute with autoscaling and active-CPU pricing so you only pay for the time your code runs. Any HTTP stack works, including Go, Rails, Spring Boot, Express, Laravel, and PHP, as long as the server listens on $PORT, with each git push producing a fresh preview URL.

title: Adversarial Pragmatics for AI Safety Evaluation: A Benchmark for Instruction Conflict, Embedded Commands, and Policy Ambiguity
blurb: A benchmark for evaluating LLM safety judgments under instruction conflict, embedded commands, and policy ambiguity, using a linguistically controlled taxonomy of adversarial pragmatics.

title: Expo 57
blurb: Expo released SDK 57, a small, focused update that brings React Native 0.86 to Expo with no intended breaking changes from 0.85. The release tracks React Native's new model of shipping a non-breaking version roughly every second release, and signals a possible shift toward faster, optional Expo upgrades applied with a single `npx expo install expo@latest --fix`.

title: ZCode – Harness for GLM-5.2
blurb: ZCode is an agentic coding harness optimized for GLM-5.2, combining AI agents with existing tools to plan, code, review, and deploy across 20+ integrations. It supports Goals for continuous planning and verification, can be steered from WeChat, Feishu, or Telegram, and ships tiered GLM Coding plans that scale usage from lightweight iteration to high-volume workloads.

title: Quantum vs. Classical Machine Learning: A Unified Empirical Comparison
blurb: A new empirical study compared seven quantum and classical machine learning model pairs across supervised learning and reinforcement learning tasks. The quantum models did not beat their classical counterparts on prediction accuracy, policy stability, or training time, though the authors note QML still shows promise for noise filtering and controlling false positives.

title: Skills Are Not Islands: Measuring Dependency and Risk in Agent Skill Supply Chains
blurb: Analyzing 1.43M agent skills as dependency-bearing supply chains, this paper finds hidden dependencies and malicious skills that per-skill inspection misses.

title: Enterprise Tech In, Shell Out (Progress Kemp LoadMaster Uninitialized Heap to Pre-Auth RCE CVE-2026-8037)
blurb: watchTowr details CVE-2026-8037, a pre-auth RCE in Progress Kemp LoadMaster reachable by anyone who can hit its API.

title: ChatGPT developer OpenAI said to discuss offering U.S. government a 5% stake: FT
blurb: OpenAI has discussed offering the U.S. government a 5% equity stake as part of a proposal to share AI-generated wealth with the public, the Financial Times reported, modeled on Alaska's Permanent Fund. The early-stage idea would likely require Congressional approval and comes as OpenAI, which confidentially filed draft IPO paperwork in June, weighs delaying its listing until 2027.

title: Anthropic Economic Index June 2026 Report
blurb: Anthropic's June 2026 Economic Index report finds that Claude usage now mirrors the rhythms of daily life, with work queries dropping on weekends, news requests peaking in the morning, and tax questions surging near filing deadlines. The report also shows that more compute correlates with more valuable outputs, and previews the first results from the Anthropic Economic Index Survey linking self-reported expectations to actual usage data.

title: A peek into Reddit's anti-spam internals
blurb: A 2021 Reddit glitch briefly exposed the site's automated spam-removal internals, replacing the generic "Removed: Auto" label with the raw signals behind each decision. The captured screenshots reveal a per-comment scoring system that weighs account age, karma, report counts, a Perspective API spam probability, email domain, TLS fingerprints, and network organization to flag likely spam.

title: Can Agents Generalize to the Open World? Unveiling the Fragility of Static Training in Tool Use
blurb: LLM agents trained on static benchmarks degrade sharply under real-world shifts in queries and tools; the authors propose perturbation-augmented fine-tuning to harden them.

title: Sony to end physical PlayStation game disc sales in 2028
blurb: Sony will stop producing physical discs for all new PlayStation games starting January 2028, shifting new releases to digital-only sales through the PlayStation Store and other retailers. The move follows digital downloads reaching 85% of full-game sales on PS4 and PS5, with games released before the cutoff still available on disc.

title: SoftBank Plans to Rent AI Compute in US at 10-Gigawatt Scale
blurb: SoftBank will start renting AI computing capacity to US companies next fiscal year, tapping a growing pipeline of data centers targeting 10 gigawatts of scale. The move puts SoftBank and its telecom unit in direct competition with GPU-cloud providers like CoreWeave and Nebius, extending its AI infrastructure push beyond stakes in OpenAI and chip designer Arm.

title: Google testing controversial webcam-based reCAPTCHA that asks for a hand scan to prove you're human — testers beat it with a stock photo
blurb: Google is testing a webcam-based reCAPTCHA that turns on a user's camera and asks them to wave or hold up an open palm, mapping 21 hand landmarks to decide whether a real person is present. Testers defeated it within days by feeding a static stock photo through OBS Virtual Camera, clearing the check with no live person or video and exposing a biometric step that adds friction for ordinary users while barely resisting attackers.

title: Qualcomm Linux 2.0
blurb: Qualcomm released Linux 2.0, a unified production Linux distribution for its Dragonwing edge and IoT chips, built on the Linux 6.18 LTS kernel with PREEMPT_RT real-time support and Yocto Project 6.0. The release collapses the previous Base and Custom variants into a single codebase with modular overlays, OSTree over-the-air updates, and public GitHub repos, targeting edge AI cameras, industrial controllers, and ROS2 robots.

title: Base's Two-Hour Outage Exposes Single-Sequencer Risk
blurb: Base went down for nearly two hours after its single sequencer processed one invalid block, halting all blocks until operators manually restarted nodes.

title: Why an idempotency key isn’t an idempotency guarantee
blurb: An idempotency key only prevents duplicate work if the system also gets four assumptions right: the claim it makes, the intent behind the request, the memory that persists it, and the boundary it protects. The author walks through a double-charge incident where a payment retry fired after a 2-second timeout, showing that a timeout is a third outcome distinct from success or failure and that a key alone does not close the gap.

title: Apple plans five new iPhones through 2027, eyes Chinese-made chips amid foldable push, reports say
blurb: Apple plans at least five new iPhone models through the first half of 2027, including its first foldable, with production targets raised to about 10 million foldable units this year. Its purchasing power let it secure components for 80 million phones and exceed 220 million in 2026 output, while Chinese rivals cut targets below 100 million amid an AI-driven memory shortage.

title: Recent OpenAI research has demonstrated the ability of LLMs to solve frontier problems in mathematics
blurb: Researchers built a pipeline using GPT-5.5 Pro and Claude Opus 4.8 that resolved nine previously open math problems, including four from the COLT open problem list, one from FOCS, and four in commutative algebra. The result pushes LLMs past benchmark-solving into original research territory, suggesting frontier models can now close problems that specialists had left unresolved at top theory venues.

title: Nvidia promises to financially backstop young cloud providers like Firmus that rent out its AI chips, in exchange for a revenue share through a new program (Phoebe Liu/The Information)
blurb: Nvidia launched a program to financially backstop young cloud providers like Firmus that rent out its AI chips, guaranteeing their revenue in exchange for a cut of the proceeds. The arrangement uses Nvidia's balance sheet to expand the pool of buyers for its expensive accelerators, propping up demand while giving the chipmaker a path to recoup some of that spending.

title: Anthropic touts new Claude Sonnet 5 model range, offering performance ‘close to that of Opus 4.8, but at lower prices’ – here’s what users can expect
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, its most agentic Sonnet model yet, with performance close to Opus 4.8 at lower prices and adjustable effort levels to trade cost against capability. Sonnet 5 scored 63.2% on SWE-bench Pro for agentic coding, up from Sonnet 4.6's 58.1% and closing the gap on Opus 4.8's 69.2%, while adding autonomous browser and terminal use over longer stretches.

title: NASA announces new lander contracts for Moon Base Phase One
blurb: NASA awarded four new commercial lunar lander contracts worth nearly $600M under Phase One of its Moon Base program, targeting flights no earlier than 2028. Astrobotic won two awards for its Peregrine landers, while Intuitive Machines and Firefly Aerospace each took one, extending the phased infrastructure buildout meant to support a permanent human presence ahead of future Artemis surface missions.

title: Scientists React to the Lab-Made, Yet Lifelike, SpudCell
blurb: Scientists created SpudCell, a lab-made structure that can grow, feed, divide, and compete with other cells, blurring the line between chemistry and biology. Its lifelike behavior has researchers debating how synthetic biology should define life, since the cell reproduces core functions of living organisms without deriving from any existing lineage.

title: Apple's Hide My Email service reportedly reveals users' actual email addresses with little effort — Cupertino has seemingly known about the problem for a year but has yet to fix it
blurb: Apple's Hide My Email, the alias feature bundled with paid iCloud plans, can reportedly be cracked to expose users' real addresses with a 100% success rate, according to 404 Media. Researcher Tyler Murphy flagged the flaw in June 2025 and says Apple's March 2026 fix did not resolve it, leaving up to 10 million subscribers exposed by a company that markets itself on privacy.

title: Autoresearch: The feedback loop behind self-improving agents
blurb: Introspection co-founder Roland Gavrilescu describes autoresearch as an "outer loop" where agents maintain and improve the primary system using evals, feedback signals, and human input rather than one-shot execution. His argument is that the loop itself becomes the product, and that autonomous software factories must first learn from human decisions before operating without them.

title: Trouble keeps finding Supermicro as strange server shipments attract police attention in Taiwan and Singapore
blurb: Supermicro is again under scrutiny as police in Taiwan and Singapore investigate unusual server shipments potentially tied to sanctions evasion. The Register reports the probes add to a long pattern of legal and regulatory trouble for the company, from past accounting problems to questions over whether its hardware is reaching restricted buyers.

title: Devin Fusion
blurb: Cognition released Devin Fusion, a multi-model harness that maintains frontier and Fable 5-level performance at 35% lower cost on FrontierCode, its new coding benchmark measuring both correctness and quality. The system runs a frontier agent alongside a cheaper "sidekick" model, delegating routine work and routing dynamically mid-session while the main agent handles planning, ambiguity, and final review. It is available in preview at app.devin.ai.

title: Claude Sonnet 5.0 heads straight down the middle of the road to dodge controversy
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5.0, positioning it as a safer, cheaper model that steps back from the cybersecurity capabilities its predecessors leaned into. The Register frames the update as a deliberately cautious release, trading headline-grabbing offensive-security features for lower cost and reduced controversy, a signal that Anthropic is prioritizing enterprise trust over benchmark bravado.

title: Apple Lobbies US to Buy Chinese-Made Memory Chips
blurb: Apple is negotiating to buy memory chips from Chinese makers ChangXin Memory Technologies and Yangtze Memory Technologies to blunt the impact of a global memory shortage. The move would deepen Apple's reliance on Chinese suppliers at a politically sensitive moment, testing US tolerance for sourcing critical components from firms Washington has worked to keep off the domestic supply chain.

title: Why jet engines aren't made in China
blurb: China has spent roughly fifty years failing to produce military and commercial jet engines at parity with the West, and the author argues this failure is diagnostic rather than incidental. Jet engines demand long-term reliability, manufacturing consistency, slow iteration, and internationally enforced certification, which neutralize China's usual advantages in cheap capital, skilled labor, and speed-to-scale that let it dominate more mature technologies.

title: Amazon has enough satellites to launch its Starlink competitor
blurb: Amazon deployed 396 satellites for its Amazon Leo service, enough to support continuous coverage across initial latitudes and keep the Starlink competitor on track for its mid-2026 commercial launch. Early performance will lag SpaceX, which now runs over 10,000 satellites at 200Mbps median speeds, and Amazon still has to launch most of its planned 3,232-satellite constellation amid Blue Origin delays.

title: The Rise and Fall of Google's Privacy Sandbox
blurb: Google retired most Privacy Sandbox APIs in October 2025, ending nearly five years of work on a privacy-preserving alternative to cross-site tracking, and this paper's weekly crawls of the top 10,000 sites show adoption had already stagnated long before the announcement. Most APIs saw use by only a handful of actors with steadily declining activity, leaving privacy-preserving interest-based advertising an unsolved problem.

title: Taiwan Detains Super Micro Workers in China Chip Smuggling Probe
blurb: Taiwanese prosecutors detained two Super Micro employees and raided the company's local offices over alleged shipments of Nvidia chips to China. The probe signals tightening enforcement of US export controls at the supply-chain level, where Taiwan-based intermediaries route restricted AI hardware into China despite the ban.

title: The Anthropic Fable Ban Is Over. The Battle Over How to Tame AI Has Just Begun.
blurb: Washington lifted its ban on Anthropic's Fable model, ending a standoff that had kept the system out of federal use. The reversal exposes deep divisions in the US over how to regulate frontier AI, with the WSJ arguing the fight over oversight rules is only beginning as agencies, lawmakers, and labs stake out competing positions.

title: Anthropic is bringing back Claude Fable 5 globally after US lifts export control order — where can enterprises access it?
blurb: Anthropic restored global access to Claude Fable 5 after the U.S. Department of Commerce withdrew the emergency export controls it issued on June 12, which had forced the company to suspend the model days after launch. Fable 5 is back across Claude.ai, the Claude Platform, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork, with AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Foundry re-enablement pending and cybersecurity sibling Mythos 5 still limited to vetted U.S. organizations.

title: Together AI raises 800 million dollars in Series C as open-source cloud demand surges
blurb: Together AI raised $800M in a Series C led by Aramco Ventures, valuing the open-source AI cloud platform at over $8B, up from roughly $3B in February 2025. The company says it now generates more than $1B in annual bookings and will use the capital to expand compute infrastructure and its inference engine, with Nvidia, Vista Equity Partners, and General Catalyst among the backers.

title: Exclusive: Lunar Forge Unveils Plans to Build Lunar Infrastructure Out of Dust
blurb: Lunar Forge emerged from stealth with a plan to laser-sinter lunar regolith into construction material, targeting housing and shielding for the Moon's fission reactors. The startup says building in-situ sidesteps the roughly $1M/kg cost of shipping materials from Earth, and its lab samples hit compressive strengths of 200 to 345 MPa, well above concrete's 20 to 30 MPa.

title: Blue Origin accelerates LC-36 recovery via hybrid plan
blurb: Blue Origin is racing to repair Launch Complex-36 at Cape Canaveral after last month's New Glenn anomaly, with CEO Dave Limp still targeting a return to flight before the end of 2026 despite forecasts of a 12- to 18-month stand-down. Early analysis points to the booster's aft section as the anomaly's origin, and the company is adopting a new hybrid concept of operations for future launches.

title: Google might be testing Gemini Flash upgrade on LM Arena
blurb: An unannounced Gemini Flash checkpoint on LM Arena appears a step above the app's current version, and Google's Arena listings have reliably preceded launches.

title: SAP Restricts Hiring, Travel to Fund ‘Significant’ AI Push
blurb: SAP will restrict hiring and travel to redirect spending toward a "significant" AI development push, as Europe's largest software company works to fend off new competitors. The cost cuts free up resources for AI features across SAP's enterprise software line, a defensive move as rivals target its core market with AI-native products.

title: Tesla hires 17-year Intel veteran responsible for billion-dollar fab startups — Gary Jiang likely chosen to oversee fab efforts for Terafab's licensing of 14A
blurb: Tesla hired Gary Jiang, a 17-year Intel veteran who most recently transferred Intel's 18A process from its Oregon development fab to high-volume production in Arizona, as the first publicly identified senior leader for Musk's Terafab chip project. The joint effort between Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI still lacks a formal head, but Jiang brings hands-on experience running billion-dollar fab startups as it reportedly licenses Intel's 14A node.

title: AI researchers trick chatbots into sharing how to make cocaine as long as they believe a user is wearing a green shirt — 'CoT Forgery' exploit spurs LLMs to divulge forbidden info by faking trusted chains of thought
blurb: A new paper called "Prompt Injection as Role Confusion" shows that LLMs judge a prompt's authority by its writing style rather than the role tags meant to separate trusted commands from untrusted data, letting attackers forge fake chains of thought the model treats as its own reasoning. The technique, dubbed CoT Forgery, lifted jailbreak success from near zero to roughly 60% across every model tested and won a 2025 OpenAI red-teaming contest.

title: Finance and Tech Cut 28,000 Jobs Monthly as AI Takes Hold
blurb: Finance and tech companies are cutting a combined 28,000 jobs monthly as AI adoption reshapes hiring, with the impact showing up first in slower recruitment, unreplaced departures, and rising attrition rather than mass layoffs. Bloomberg reports these two sectors are the earliest to visibly absorb AI's labor effects, offering a preview of how the technology may quietly reduce headcount across the broader economy.

title: Google Loses EU Top Court Fight Over €4.1 Billion Android Fine
blurb: Google lost its appeal against a €4.1 billion ($4.7 billion) EU antitrust fine, with the bloc's top court upholding regulators' finding that the company abused Android's market dominance. The ruling ends a long-running challenge to the 2018 penalty over deals that forced phone makers to preinstall Search and Chrome, cementing one of the largest antitrust fines in EU history.

title: Weave Robotics launches Isaac 1, a $7,999 home robot with Fall 2026 deliveries
blurb: Weave Robotics launched Isaac 1, a $7,999 wheeled home robot that folds laundry, resets rooms, and navigates autonomously, with teleoperators stepping in when a task stalls. The San Francisco startup built its own actuators, safety systems, and swappable fabric shells, and plans first California deliveries in fall 2026 with capabilities expanding over the air.

title: Google loses final appeal over $4.7 billion EU Android antitrust fine
blurb: The EU's top court upheld a record €4.1 billion ($4.67 billion) antitrust fine against Google, ending a decade-long fight over Android agreements that forced device makers to preinstall Chrome and Google Search as defaults. The ruling closes the 2018 case but not Google's EU troubles, which now include Digital Markets Act probes over self-preferencing in search and Play Store payment steering.

title: Godot will no longer accept AI-authored code contributions
blurb: The Godot Foundation will bar AI-authored code, AI-submitted pull requests, and AI-generated text from human communication, after months of maintainers being overwhelmed by low-effort "slop" contributions. The project argues that AI cannot take responsibility for its output and that heavy AI users often don't understand their own code well enough to fix it, so new policies will add barriers to low-effort PRs and prioritize mentoring accountable human contributors.

title: Figma's design agent, now with custom tools and greater context
blurb: Figma expanded its canvas-based design agent with custom tools, skills, and richer context about how a team works. Designers can now prompt the agent to build reusable generative plugins using PropsKit so they feel native to Figma, plus create custom shader effects and fills, without needing a developer or a separate dev setup.

title: X now offers an MCP server to make its platform easier for AI tools to use
blurb: X released a hosted Model Context Protocol server that lets AI tools like Claude, Cursor, and Grok Build connect to the X API using a user's own account permissions, eliminating the integration and authentication work developers previously had to build themselves. The server adds no new capabilities and blocks Write access to prevent autonomous posting, positioning X as a real-time data source alongside GitHub, Slack, and Stripe in the growing roster of official MCP endpoints.

title: MGX Raises $49 Billion for AI Deals
blurb: MGX raised $49B for one of the largest funds ever dedicated to AI deals, vaulting the two-year-old Abu Dhabi firm into the top tier of global technology investors. The vehicle gives MGX substantial firepower to back frontier labs, data centers, and infrastructure at a moment when capital, not model quality, increasingly sets the pace of AI buildout.

title: Bending Spoons defies SaaS slump, surges 40% on first day of trading
blurb: Bending Spoons closed up nearly 40% on its first trading day, hitting $40.50 against a $29 IPO price and a $25.7B market cap, more than double its last private valuation. The Milan-based firm buys aging brands like AOL, Evernote, and Vimeo, then cuts costs and raises prices to turn them profitable, posting $601M in Q1 revenue and $27.4M in net income after a loss a year earlier.

title: Meta, like SpaceX, looks to turn excess AI compute into cash
blurb: Meta is developing a cloud infrastructure business to sell access to its AI compute and models, pitting it against AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure, according to Bloomberg. The move mirrors xAI's recent compute leases and aims to monetize part of Meta's $182.9B AI infrastructure commitment, including a Manhattan-sized Ohio data center that has yet to yield a material standalone revenue line.

title: Draped Surfaces: A Contour-Adaptive Interface Overlaid on the Physical Environment for Mixed Reality Workspaces
blurb: This paper introduces CAMEO, a mixed-reality interface that drapes virtual windows onto physical surfaces instead of floating them in cockpit-style layouts that occlude the real world. In two controlled studies, draping cut hand-movement detours when reaching for nearby objects, and the resulting window deformation did not significantly hurt text legibility compared to flat mid-air surfaces.

title: Hanami 3.0: In Full Bloom
blurb: Hanami 3.0 lands for Ruby with integrated mailers, built-in i18n, Minitest support, and faster-by-default apps.

title: Sony kills physical PlayStation games. The era of discs comes to an end for Team Blue
blurb: Sony will stop producing physical discs for new PlayStation games starting January 2028, making all titles released after that date digital-only through the PlayStation Store or retailers. Existing discs and current consoles keep working, but the move formalizes a shift Sony had already signaled with the all-digital PS5 in 2020, the disc-free PS5 Slim in 2023, and the driveless PS5 Pro in 2025.

title: Blue Origin outlines new launch pad approach as it pushes to return New Glenn to flight
blurb: Blue Origin plans to return New Glenn to flight by the end of 2026 using a new horizontal/vertical hybrid launch process instead of rebuilding the transporter/erector destroyed in the May 28 pad explosion. The rocket will roll out horizontally, be raised by crane, and have its payload attached on the pad, a recovery that would take roughly seven months while the cause, traced to the first stage's aft section, remains under investigation.

title: The European Court of Justice rules that Google's earlier defeat against a €4.1B European Commission penalty over abusing Android market power should stand (Bloomberg)
blurb: The European Court of Justice upheld a €4.1B ($4.7B) antitrust fine against Google, ending the company's long-running appeal over how it used Android to entrench its search dominance. The ruling finalizes a penalty the European Commission first issued in 2018 for forcing device makers to preinstall Google apps, closing one of the EU's landmark cases against the company.

title: Virginia county asks all employees, including schools, to conserve power due to AI-driven electricity price hikes — state's 400-plus data centers steadily increasing demand, grid expansion, and pricing
blurb: Henrico County, Virginia asked all employees, including those in schools and social services, to conserve power as data-center demand drives repeated electricity rate hikes. Government entities in the state face a 24.9% increase next month, part of a broader squeeze from Northern Virginia's 400-plus data centers, which push utilities like Dominion Energy into costly grid buildouts that critics say land on households.

title: QUANTUM SYSTEMS lève 1,2 milliard de dollars : la bataille des drones est terminée, celle des plateformes de défense commence
blurb: Quantum Systems raised $1.2B in a Series D at a valuation near $8B, led by investors including Blackstone, Advent, and Fidelity. The German drone maker is repositioning as a European "neo prime" defense platform, shifting its strategic value from hardware to MOSAIC UXS, software that connects drones, robots, sensors, and command systems in one architecture.

title: Measuring the Gap Between Human and LLM Research Ideas
blurb: LLM-generated research ideas cluster narrowly around bridge-like opportunities and synthesis methods, staying systematically shifted from the broader spread of human research taste.

title: US Government allows Anthropic limited release of AI model that sparked cybersecurity concerns
blurb: The US government cleared Anthropic to redeploy its Mythos 5 cybersecurity model to a small group of vetted cyber defenders and infrastructure providers, reversing an export block imposed earlier in June over national security concerns. The revised license excludes Fable 5, the weaker variant still awaiting general-release approval, and underscores the absence of a consistent US regulatory framework for frontier AI.

title: The inside scoop on alerting changes in Kubernetes Monitoring
blurb: Grafana Cloud is consolidating Kubernetes Monitoring alerts onto Grafana-managed alerting, so reinstalls may silently break notifications routed through the old Mimir Alertmanager.

title: Cost Attribution in Discord's API
blurb: Discord built cost attribution into its API by extending its existing application profiling tooling, allowing it to allocate hosting spend to individual endpoints and product features without changing its Kubernetes deployment topology. The approach solves a common observability gap for teams running one shared codebase across many deployments, where cloud providers can split costs by deployment but not by the feature-level code each worker executes.

title: DRAM it! Cheap PCs being priced out of existence as memory cost bites
blurb: Surging DRAM prices are pushing entry-level PCs toward extinction as memory costs consume a growing share of each build's bill of materials. With AI datacenter demand soaking up supply and manufacturers prioritizing high-margin server chips, budget machines face price hikes that erode the low end of the market.

title: The best thing that's ever happened for multiplayer games?
blurb: Amazon GameLift Servers now include AWS network bandwidth at no extra charge, eliminating egress costs that could run over $1.6M/month at scale.

title: Data Center Firm Switch Seeks $2 Billion in Funding Round
blurb: Switch is raising about $2B in a private funding round led by Andreessen Horowitz, according to people familiar with the talks. The data center operator's raise reflects investor demand for the physical infrastructure behind the AI buildout, where capital is flowing toward power, land, and capacity rather than models alone.

title: Supersonic flight returning to US after half-century ban
blurb: The FAA has proposed replacing its 1973 ban on overland supersonic flight with a noise-based limit, allowing civil aircraft to exceed Mach 1 over US land as long as ground noise stays below a set threshold. The move follows a June 2025 Trump executive order and bets that newer designs can suppress the sonic boom that broke windows and drew thousands of complaints in the Concorde era; the FAA aims to finalize the rules by mid-2027.

title: Apple Fixes WebKit Flaws in iOS and macOS, With Help From AI Tools
blurb: Apple patched four WebKit vulnerabilities found with AI tools including Claude and Codex, shipping the fixes ahead of the usual iOS release cycle.

title: OpenAI reportedly wants all AI companies to give the US government a stake in their businesses
blurb: Sam Altman has reportedly proposed that OpenAI and rival labs including Google, Anthropic, xAI, and Meta each hand the US government a roughly 5% equity stake, according to the Financial Times. The plan, still in early talks and requiring congressional approval, would channel shares into sovereign funds like the Alaska Permanent Fund, echoing the administration's recent 10% stake in Intel.

title: Anonymous Researcher Drops 0-day 'Exploitarium' Repo
blurb: An anonymous researcher published "Exploitarium," a public GitHub repo collecting working exploit code for multiple unpatched 0-day vulnerabilities. The dump hands attackers ready-made tooling before vendors can ship fixes, reviving the long-running debate over whether full public disclosure pressures companies to patch faster or simply arms opportunists during the exposure window.

title: Opera’s new Paste Protect feature stops the clipboard attack your antivirus can’t catch
blurb: Opera's new Paste Protect feature adds native browser defense against ClickFix and clipboard-hijacking attacks, screening copied content in real time for malicious scripts.

title: Chip Off The Old Block
blurb: Scott Alexander argues that having a son gives parents an unexpected chance to see their own youth reflected back, quoting a passage from Song of Hiawatha that captures the feeling. He notes his own childhood train obsession reappearing in his son as evidence that traits pass down in ways too specific and strange to be explained by deliberate parenting.

title: Final Atlas 5 Amazon Leo mission launches
blurb: An Atlas 5 launched July 2 from Cape Canaveral carrying 29 Amazon Leo satellites, the final flight of the vehicle to carry a satellite payload. The mission pushes Amazon's broadband constellation past 390 satellites and closes out eight operational Atlas 5 flights, as Amazon shifts remaining deployments to ULA's delayed Vulcan Centaur ahead of an initial service rollout later this year.

title: Together AI raises $800M at $8.3B valuation as enterprises ditch closed models for open-source
blurb: Together AI raised $800M at an $8.3B valuation in a Series C led by Aramco Ventures, up from $3.3B in February 2025. The company runs cloud infrastructure for deploying open-source models like DeepSeek and Kimi rather than building its own, claiming up to 60x lower inference costs than closed rivals and pushing annual bookings past $1.15B.

title: The Next Paradigm
blurb: The author argues that the labs' current bet, training AIs on millions of verifiable tasks across thousands of RL environments, may reach AGI by treating the paradigm's apparent limits as engineering problems rather than fundamental ones. Sample inefficiency is a one-time training cost amortized across billions of sessions, and continual learning could become unnecessary if in-context learning keeps extending to longer horizons, making weight updates from deployment optional.

title: The "Akrites" vulnerability-mitigation project launches
blurb: Akrites is a new project that fixes vulnerabilities upstream and keeps them confidential until patches are widely deployed, citing AI-accelerated exploit development.

title: Free The Icons
blurb: Apple's macOS Tahoe forces every app icon into a uniform squircle; this post argues for restoring distinct icon shapes for usability and personality.

title: Musk-Altman Judge Enlists Mediator to Resolve AI Titans’ Battle
blurb: The judge overseeing the lawsuit between Elon Musk and Sam Altman has appointed a mediator to try to settle the dispute between their competing AI companies. The move signals the court would rather see Musk's suit against OpenAI resolved out of court than proceed to a trial that could drag on and expose internal details from both xAI and OpenAI.

title: Meta caps internal AI token spending
blurb: Meta warned roughly 6,000 employees in an internal memo that runaway AI usage is on track to cost billions in 2026, after workers burned 73.7 trillion tokens in about 30 days on tools like Anthropic's Claude. The company will kill its usage-ranking "Claudeonomics" leaderboard, roll out a centralized "AI Gateway" dashboard with formal token budgets in 2027, and steer engineers toward its own MetaCode assistant.

title: Intel expands production of photomasks in California: EUV and High-NA EUV in the focal point
blurb: Intel began construction on an expansion of its Bowers Campus in Santa Clara to boost US production of photomasks for advanced DUV, EUV, and eventually High-NA EUV chip layers. The new 107,000-square-foot facility with a Class 1 cleanroom will supply reticles for leading-edge nodes like Intel's 18A and 14A, keeping one of the industry's few in-house mask shops close to production.

title: Rocket Lab Enters the Satcom Market With $8B Iridium Deal
blurb: Rocket Lab announced an $8B cash-and-stock deal to acquire Iridium Communications, its first move into satellite services beyond launch and spacecraft manufacturing. The purchase, backed by a $3.6B bridge loan from Deutsche Bank and Wells Fargo, would more than double Rocket Lab's revenue and hand it Iridium's globally licensed L-band spectrum, which powers emergency, GPS, and aviation communications.

title: Fable is coming back: Federal government lifts export controls on Anthropic AI model
blurb: The U.S. Commerce Department lifted the export controls it placed on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models two weeks ago, clearing Anthropic to restore Fable access for all users. In a letter to co-founder Tom Brown, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said Anthropic agreed to proactively detect security risks, report malicious activity, and work with the government on release protocols for current and future models.

title: GPU-Accelerated Autorouter Handles Monstrous PCB Designs
blurb: A developer built OrthoRoute, a GPU-accelerated KiCad autorouter that lays down traces in an orthogonal Manhattan grid to handle dense PCBs with thousands of nets that stall traditional routers. It adapts PathFinder, an FPGA-routing algorithm, running the calculations on a GPU rather than a CPU, which demonstrates that GPU-accelerated PCB routing is feasible even if the tool currently serves a narrow set of users.

title: An interview with Amazon SVP of Devices Panos Panay on designing custom chips for Echo and Fire TV devices, experimenting with AI-enabled gadgets, and more (Arjun Kharpal/CNBC)
blurb: Amazon is designing custom silicon for its Echo and Fire TV devices, SVP Panos Panay told CNBC, framing in-house chips as central to the company's consumer hardware roadmap. Panay said Amazon is prioritizing chips for its "critical" devices while experimenting with AI-enabled gadgets, a strategy that mirrors Apple and Google's push to control the silicon inside their products.

title: Raja Koduri’s Oxmiq Labs raises $35M to lower the design cost of custom AI silicon
blurb: Oxmiq Labs, founded by former Intel graphics chief Raja Koduri, raised $35M in a Series A led by Fundomo and Samsung Catalyst Fund, bringing its total to $60M. The startup licenses an open GPU architecture called OxCore that bundles GPU, CPU, and a tensor engine into one IP block, aiming to become the Arm of custom AI silicon by cutting design costs that typically run into hundreds of millions.

title: Samsung details $90bn plan for South Korea’s Chungcheong region
blurb: Samsung will invest 140 trillion won, roughly $90B, over the next decade to build out displays, memory chips, batteries, and chip-packaging materials across South Korea's Chungcheong region. The bulk goes to Samsung Display's OLED expansion in Asan (67 trillion won) and Samsung Electronics' high-bandwidth memory bases in Onyang and Cheonan (56 trillion won), the chips central to Nvidia's AI accelerators.

title: NASA Praises Blue Origin’s Mishap Progress, Announces Moon Base Contracts
blurb: NASA awarded roughly $600M in new lunar delivery contracts to Astrobotic, Intuitive Machines, and Firefly, and said Blue Origin is recovering well from last month's New Glenn explosion. The vendors will fly updated versions of already-flown lander designs to support NASA's goal of monthly Moon landings, while Blue Origin remains a candidate to fly Artemis III no earlier than 2027.

title: Apple reportedly orders 10M foldable iPhone Ultra models, which could sell for around $2500
blurb: Apple has told suppliers to prepare around 10 million foldable iPhone Ultra units this year, up from a prior target of 7 to 8 million, according to Nikkei Asia. The device is expected to debut in September alongside the iPhone 18 Pro and Pro Max, with IDC projecting an average selling price near $2500 and up to $3000 for higher storage tiers.

title: Wayve undertakes $85M employee tender offer to “retain talent”
blurb: Wayve launched an $85M employee tender offer, letting staff sell equity to new and existing investors as the UK autonomous-driving startup works to retain talent. The move follows its $8.6B valuation from February's $1.2B Series D and marks Wayve's second tender offer, joining Stripe, ElevenLabs, and Revolut in using the mechanism as IPO markets stay frozen.

title: CMA consults on new requirements for Apple and Google’s mobile platforms
blurb: The UK's CMA opened a consultation on new conduct rules that would force Apple and Google to let developers steer users to cheaper off-platform payment options and cap steering fees below current app store charges. The proposal also floats requiring Apple to open NFC access, letting UK fintechs support contactless payments and future methods like stablecoins directly inside their iOS apps.

title: An EU draft proposal dated June 30 shows the bloc is weighing weaker climate impact rules for gas-powered data centers, after heavy lobbying from tech groups (Financial Times)
blurb: A leaked EU draft proposal dated June 30 shows the bloc is weighing weaker climate impact rules for gas-powered data centers following heavy lobbying from tech groups. Earlier plans requiring strict renewable energy certificates were dropped, signaling that surging AI compute demand is reshaping how Europe regulates the energy footprint of the facilities powering it.

title: How Lime went from pandemic write-off to a $167M IPO
blurb: Lime priced its Nasdaq IPO at $25 a share on June 30, raising $167 million and returning the micromobility company to public markets after its valuation crashed from $2.4 billion in 2019 to about $510 million in 2020. Uber, whose 2020 investment and in-app integration revived Lime's demand, plans to buy up to $20 million of shares in the offering.

title: Germany-based Quantum Systems, which sells surveillance drones used by Ukraine, raised $1.2B led by Blackstone, Noteus, Airbus, and Advent at an ~$8B valuation (Yazhou Sun/Bloomberg)
blurb: Quantum Systems, the Germany-based maker of surveillance drones deployed by Ukraine, raised $1.2B at a roughly $8B valuation, more than doubling its prior worth. The round was led by Blackstone, Noteus, Airbus, and Advent, underscoring how the war in Ukraine has turned European defense-tech startups into targets for large institutional and strategic investors.

title: Adobe is Buying Topaz Labs, the AI Video Enhancer
blurb: Adobe agreed to acquire Topaz Labs, the Emmy-winning maker of AI tools that upscale, sharpen, denoise, and restore images and video. The terms were undisclosed, and Adobe plans to fold Topaz's models into Firefly and Creative Cloud apps like Photoshop, Lightroom, and Premiere, gaining on-device technology that runs heavy AI video processing locally instead of in the cloud.

title: Mark Zuckerberg wants Meta to launch its own prediction market
blurb: Mark Zuckerberg has approved Meta's development of a standalone prediction market app, internally called "Arena," according to The New York Times. The Polymarket-style app would launch independent of Meta's social platforms and initially use points rather than real money, with cash betting potentially added later, as Polymarket and Kalshi push trading volumes into the tens of billions of dollars.

title: Build from anywhere with Cursor for iOS
blurb: Cursor released a native iOS app in public beta that lets developers launch and control coding agents from their phones. Users can start cloud-based agents or remote-control agents running on their computer, pick any frontier model, guide work with voice input and slash commands, and review PRs, screenshots, and logs, with lock-screen Live Activities and push notifications tracking progress.

title: Salesforce employees are confused about why the company is promoting a competitor inside Slack
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, an always-on AI agent that works inside Slack channels, and Salesforce promoted it publicly even though it competes directly with Salesforce's own Slackbot and Agentforce. The overlap is awkward but financially logical: Salesforce, which owns Slack, expects to spend $300M on Anthropic tokens this year and holds a roughly 1% stake in Anthropic, now valued at $380B.

title: Supreme Court ruling guts government's use of geofence warrants
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that the Fourth Amendment protects a phone's "location history," requiring police to obtain a warrant with probable cause before using geofence warrants that pull every device logged in a given area. Writing for the majority, Justice Kagan found the amount of data seized irrelevant, since users routinely opt into location tracking to make apps work and Google rarely discloses how that data can reach the government.

title: SpaceX Analyst Debut Set to Test $2.2 Trillion Valuation
blurb: SpaceX will get its first Wall Street analyst coverage this week, the first outside test of the $2.2 trillion valuation set at last month's record IPO. Investors have had little to go on since the debut, with few financial projections available to gauge what the stock is actually worth against the company's launch and Starlink revenue.

title: Drone maker Quantum Systems raises $1.2bn, eyes humanoids
blurb: Munich-based drone maker Quantum Systems raised $1.2B in Series D funding at an $8B valuation, co-led by Blackstone, Airbus, Noteus, and Advent. The company builds autonomous aerial and land drones and plans to use the cash to consolidate the crowded European defense market, weighing acquisitions, a possible merger with strike-drone startup Stark, and expansion into maritime and humanoid systems.

title: Higgsfield targets $5B valuation and $500M run rate as Sora folds and Runway retreats
blurb: Higgsfield is in talks to raise $300M to $500M at a $5B valuation, roughly four times what the AI video startup was worth in January 2026. The company says its annualized run rate hit $500M in June, up from $200M at the end of 2025, and that a clearer field, after Sora shut down in April and Runway pivoted to world models, helped drive the growth.

title: Amazon is designing its own AI chips for Echo, Fire TV and future devices, exec tells CNBC
blurb: Amazon designs its own end-to-end silicon for critical consumer devices like the Echo Show and Fire TV, hardware chief Panos Panay told CNBC, building on the AZ3 and AZ3 Pro chips that run AI models on-device rather than in the cloud. The custom-silicon push mirrors Apple's hardware-software integration strategy and supports Alexa+, though Amazon still buys chips from suppliers like Qualcomm.

title: Touching and Feeling the Data: A Reusable Software Pipeline for Tactile Statistical Graphs in Accessible Education
blurb: A JavaScript pipeline auto-generates 3D-printable tactile statistical graphs for blind and low-vision students in under 250ms per chart.

title: EchoStar’s satellite TV and wireless subsidiaries file for bankruptcy
blurb: EchoStar subsidiaries tied to its satellite TV and abandoned 5G businesses, including DISH DBS and DISH Wireless, filed for Chapter 11 in Texas to advance a prepackaged restructuring after selling more than $40B in spectrum to SpaceX and AT&T. The filing follows delayed AT&T sale proceeds that left DISH DBS unable to cover $2B in debt due July 1, and it leaves DISH TV, Sling TV, and Boost Mobile operations untouched.

title: Alibaba-affiliate Ant Group rushes into humanoid robots with a dozen deals in 18 months
blurb: Ant Group led a 500M yuan ($73.6M) round in humanoid robotics startup Zeroth, its 12th investment in the sector since early 2025. The Alibaba affiliate is building a portfolio spanning robot makers like Unitree and Galaxea plus parts and software suppliers, adding to a China humanoid push that also drew Nvidia to open robotics roles in Beijing, Shanghai, and Shenzhen this week.

title: X is making a fresh push for live video with new creator payouts
blurb: X launched Live Studio, a livestreaming command center in Creator Studio, and set aside $1M to reward creators who go live during the upcoming cycle. The tools let streamers schedule broadcasts, gate access to subscribers or verified accounts, and track concurrent viewers by country and device, though live streaming stays limited to $3-a-month X Premium subscribers.

title: Competition regulator ‘falls short’ in app developer consultation
blurb: The UK Competition and Markets Authority opened a consultation that could force Apple and Google to let developers steer users toward payment options outside their app stores, bypassing the 30% cut. The Coalition for App Fairness, whose members include Epic Games and Spotify, called the measures "cosmetic" and said any fees must be justified by transparent cost data before the reform meaningfully increases competition.

title: Getty Images is canceling its $3.7 billion Shutterstock merger due to UK restrictions
blurb: Getty Images terminated its $3.7B merger with Shutterstock after the UK's Competition and Markets Authority demanded Shutterstock sell its global editorial business, including its celebrity and news photo agencies. The deal had already cleared the US DoJ unconditionally, showing that American approval alone can't close a cross-border merger and foreshadowing the risk Paramount faces on its Warner Bros. Discovery deal.

title: Reward Models Can Be Too Sensitive
blurb: This paper shows many reward models are oversensitive, scoring equally good responses differently, and proposes Monte Carlo dropout discretization to cut reward hacking.

title: Oomwoo, an open-source robot vacuum you build yourself
blurb: Oomwoo is a build-it-yourself, open-source robot vacuum running Raspberry Pi, ROS 2, and 2D LiDAR, with a 3D-printed chassis and native Home Assistant integration. The project is local-first with no cloud dependency, and it is genuinely early, currently at a bare-bones v0 with a working chassis, Gazebo simulation, and manual SLAM, with hardware files, firmware, and a full bill of materials still in progress.

title: FokosDB: A strongly consistent bottomless storage database ontop of Cloudflare Durable Objects
blurb: A developer built FokosDB, a strongly consistent, bottomless-storage database on top of Cloudflare Durable Objects, and details its architecture and trade-offs.

title: New FBI Alert: Russian Intelligence Uses Signal Recovery Keys to Access Messages
blurb: The FBI and CISA updated their March 2026 advisory to warn that FSB-linked groups, now tracked as UNC5792 and UNC4221, have shifted from phishing Signal verification codes to stealing users' Backup Recovery Keys. Unlike a one-time code, a recovery key unlocks the entire message archive and keeps working even after the victim creates a new account, giving attackers persistent access to historical private and group messages.

title: Nvidia competitor Etched hits $5B valuation, $1B in sales for AI chip
blurb: Etched, an Nvidia challenger building custom inference chips, came out of stealth with $1B in booked orders and an $800M fundraise, including a $500M round in December at a $5B valuation. Its "frontier inference clusters" bundle the chips with custom racks and software to run frontier-model inference faster and cheaper, targeting the biggest cost center for AI companies serving models at scale.

title: Report links Starliner problems to overconfidence and unrealistic schedules
blurb: A NASA Office of Inspector General report released June 30 traced Boeing's long-running Starliner problems to overconfidence in heritage systems, unrealistic launch schedules, and NASA's limited access to flight simulator data. The vehicle hasn't flown since its 2024 crew test suffered thruster malfunctions that forced an uncrewed return, and the commercial crew program has lost 21% of its staff to attrition as certification for crewed missions remains uncertain.

title: Meta explores cloud infrastructure business
blurb: Meta is exploring a cloud infrastructure business to sell its AI computing power to outside customers, including competitors, according to Bloomberg. The move would put Meta in direct competition with AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud, monetizing the massive GPU capacity it has been building out for its own AI ambitions.

title: WhatsApp usernames are already raising impersonation red flags
blurb: WhatsApp began rolling out username reservations this week ahead of a broader launch later this year, letting people message each other by handle instead of phone number. The feature is already drawing impersonation concerns, with handles like "rbi_verify" and "indiamodi" still claimable and India's IT ministry warning WhatsApp that usernames could increase phishing and digital-arrest scams in its largest market.

title: Working With AI: A Concrete Example
blurb: htmx creator Carson Gross walks through a real hyperscript parser bug to illustrate "The Sorcerer's Apprentice problem," where developers who lean on AI lose the ability to understand and fix their own systems. Claude correctly found the root cause of the parsing regression, but Gross argues the episode shows why engineers must still grasp the underlying code rather than defer blindly to the tool's output.

title: Voltage Glitching Attack Bypasses Safety Checks in PX4 Autopilot Flight Controllers
blurb: Security researchers at UC demonstrated a voltage glitching attack that bypasses safety checks on the PX4 Autopilot flight controller, letting them skip or inject control instructions on STMicroelectronics STM32 chips widely used in research, hobbyist, and commercial UAVs. The fault injection can alter failsafe mechanisms and disrupt instruction execution, though it currently requires physical access to the target device.
