blurb: LLVM-Bench is the first large-scale benchmark for LLVM compiler issue resolution, containing 423 real-world validated tasks paired with LLVM-Gym, a platform that automates issue reproduction, patch application, compiler builds, and test execution. Current LLMs struggle, with patch invalidity and build failures dominating, though LLVM-Ens, a lightweight ensemble that merges and filters patches from diverse models and agents, reaches a resolution rate of up to 21.99%.

title: Towards Better Linux Kernel Fault Localization: Leveraging Contrastive Reasoning and Hierarchical Context Analysis
blurb: CoHiKer is an LLM-based fault localization technique for the Linux kernel that pairs contrastive reasoning over mutated passing and failing test cases with hierarchical context analysis that narrows the search from files to methods. Against five state-of-the-art baselines, it improves Top-1 accuracy by up to 26.07% at the file level and 56.85% at the method level while cutting token consumption by up to 8.84% and 28.9% respectively.

title: Beyond Static Prompts: Building Scale-Proof, Polymorphic Multi-Agent Systems with Google's ADK
blurb: This post presents Context-Aware Polymorphic Schema Validation, an architecture built on Google's ADK and Gemini Flash that decouples an agent's reasoning from its structural data requirements. Instead of preloading every JSON schema into the system prompt, it uses a centralized metadata registry to inject context and enforce schema validation at runtime, cutting token costs and reducing the attention diffusion that leads agents to mix fields from inactive schemas.

title: DigitalCoach: Communication and Grounding Gaps in Human and Agentic Computer Use Coaching
blurb: DigitalCoach is a multimodal dataset of 72 expert-novice software coaching sessions, spanning 22,752 dialogue turns grounded in 28.1 hours of screen and input recordings across five applications. Evaluated on it, current models give more direct instructions but fewer explanations, error diagnoses, and knowledge-check questions than humans, and their coaching stays poorly grounded in visual context, leaving learners passively following steps.

title: x402 Agentic Commerce Hits 500k Daily Transactions
blurb: Payments using the x402 protocol rose 5x in June to roughly 500k daily transactions, driven mostly by pay-per-use AI inference.

title: How ChatGPT Actually Picks Sources (I Read the Network Traffic, Not the Outputs)
blurb: The author reverse-engineered how ChatGPT selects sources by reading two days of raw network traffic rather than analyzing finished answers, logging roughly 1,240 source records across a few dozen searches. The traffic exposes ChatGPT's internal labels, including the result_source field that stamps each result with providers like SERP, Labrador, Bright Data, and Oxylabs. The author separates high-confidence structural findings about which fields the engine uses from the low-confidence percentages drawn from a small, SaaS-heavy query sample.

title: CarbonSix, which is developing AI automation and robotic intelligence tech for manufacturing, raised a $40M Series A co-led by DSC Investment and LB Investment (FinSMEs)
blurb: CarbonSix, a San Francisco-based physical AI company building automation and robotic intelligence for manufacturing, raised a $40M Series A co-led by DSC Investment and LB Investment. The round, roughly KRW 60 billion, will fund development of systems that bring robotic autonomy to factory production lines.

title: Autonomous Scientific Discovery via Iterative Meta-Reflection
blurb: DiscoPER is an LLM-powered framework for open-ended scientific discovery that generates and runs code to explore datasets without predefined research questions, validating every proposed finding with statistical testing. On iNatDisco, a new multimodal ecological benchmark, it recovers 8 of 9 known patterns at a 72.7% hypothesis support rate using second-order meta-reflection that treats prior discoveries as data to redirect the search.

title: Cheap Code, Costly Judgment: A Case Study on Governable Agentic Software Engineering
blurb: This paper argues that generative AI shifts software engineering from a discipline of scarce implementation effort to one of abundant, low-cost code, making the central problem how to keep AI-mediated development inspectable, correctable, and maintainable. Drawing on a 12-week case study in which one expert engineer used frontier coding agents to build a document accessibility system, the authors propose a "governance conversion" model, where high-velocity agentic work surfaces recurring structural failures that engineering judgment then converts into durable controls.

title: 가트너 “에이전틱 AI에 SaaS 시장 재편…기존 업체는 위기, 서비스 기업은 기회”
blurb: Gartner forecasts that agentic AI will put up to $234B of enterprise application spending at stake through 2030, roughly 20% of projected enterprise SaaS spend. The firm calls the shift "agentic arbitrage": as agents execute work across systems, users interact with software interfaces less directly, weakening the link between seat growth and revenue. Gartner argues incumbents relying on dashboards and per-user pricing face erosion, while service and platform vendors offering outcome-based, cross-domain agentic layers stand to capture the freed-up budget.

title: KnowledgeDebugger -- an Exploration Tool for Knowledge Localization and Editing in Transformers
blurb: KnowledgeDebugger is a GUI-based exploration tool for locating and editing factual knowledge in Transformers, giving no-code access to the methods in the EasyEdit library. Inspired by LM-Debugger, it targets the early, single-sample phase of interpretability work, letting researchers test knowledge localization and editing hypotheses before moving to statistically robust experiments.

title: Z.ai launches ZCode to challenge Cursor, Claude Code and GitHub Copilot in AI coding
blurb: Z.ai launched ZCode, a free agent-first coding environment tuned for its GLM-5.2 model, with remote steering from WeChat, Feishu, and Telegram.

title: SenseWalk: Agent-Based Semantic Trajectory Simulation Powered by Large Language Models in Zoned Environments
blurb: SenseWalk is an interactive system that simulates semantic human trajectories using LLM-powered agents in zoned environments. It pairs LLMs with the social force model to balance physical plausibility and semantic coherence, and was evaluated through a quantitative experiment and a 12-person user study assessing usefulness and efficiency.

title: Meta explores cloud infrastructure business
blurb: Meta is exploring a cloud infrastructure business that would sell access to its AI computing power to outside customers, including competitors, according to Bloomberg. The move would position Meta against Amazon, Microsoft, and Google in the cloud market and help offset the cost of the massive data center buildout it has committed to for training frontier models.

title: Disentangling Speaker and Language Effects in Cross-Lingual Speaker Verification for Iberian Languages
blurb: A bilingual same-speaker evaluation set for five Iberian languages shows language mismatch, not speaker variability, drives most cross-lingual speaker verification degradation.

title: Sakana AI and 360 Security Launch AI Tools to Fill the Gap Anthropic's Export Ban Created
blurb: Sakana AI and 360 Security launched AI tools positioned as alternatives to Anthropic's Mythos and Fable 5 models, both restricted under the US export ban now in its third week. Tokyo-based Sakana released Fugu, a seven-billion-parameter orchestrator that routes tasks across external models to match Fable 5 benchmarks without training a frontier model, while Beijing's 360 Security introduced Tulongfeng, a vulnerability-discovery tool it claims rivals Mythos.

title: A field experiment of social influence and behavioral contagion with bots on Reddit
blurb: This paper reports a Reddit field experiment testing whether symbolic awards from apparent humans and bots could nudge users toward more activity and influence, using rationales that praised logic, emotion, or moral integrity, or cited a random lottery. Awards failed to boost activity and downstream impact, and bot awards with a lottery rationale sometimes reduced them, though they did encourage direct communication between users.

title: AIEWF Daily Dispatch: Autoresearch and the tension between AI and human agency
blurb: Latent Space's AI Engineer World's Fair dispatch covers "autoresearch" loops and the debate over how much of the engineering outer loop humans should keep.

title: Child Safety in Generative AI: An Expert-Guided and Incident-Grounded Evaluation Framework
blurb: This paper proposes a child safety evaluation framework for generative AI that combines expert-guided risk factors with real-world AI incident data to build synthetic test sets targeting child-specific harms. Applied to the education domain, it found that current Llama Guard models struggle to detect unsafe user prompts from younger users.

title: How Much Do RF Drone Benchmarks Overstate? A Controlled Study and Theory of Data Leakage in UAV Signal Identification
blurb: This paper shows RF drone-identification benchmarks inflate accuracy through segment-level data leakage, with DroneRF macro-F1 collapsing from 0.74 to chance under honest evaluation.

title: Does ERC-8004 Form an Agent-to-Agent Trust Network?
blurb: Early ERC-8004 on-chain data shows it functions as an identity and reputation layer, not a real agent-to-agent trust network, with ~95% of agents inactive.

title: Popping the GPU Bubble
blurb: GPUs often sit idle during model inference, not for lack of work but because the CPU hasn't told them what to do next yet. Moondream calls this a GPU bubble: each token's decode loop pays a fixed CPU housekeeping cost while the GPU waits. Their Photon runtime hides the gap with pipelined decoding, launching the next token's GPU work while the CPU is still committing the last one, so the forwards run back-to-back instead of taking turns.

title: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna
blurb: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6, a three-model family spanning Sol, the flagship, Terra, a cheaper mid-tier, and Luna, the fastest and most cost-efficient. It is the first launch OpenAI is treating as High capability in both cybersecurity and biological and chemical risk, so general availability is gated behind a limited preview to a small set of trusted partners shared with the U.S. government. The models can find vulnerabilities and exploit fragments but cannot yet run autonomous end-to-end attacks, and the bigger signal is that frontier releases now ship under government coordination and a heavier safety stack.

title: Meta pops 8% as company makes cloud push to sell excess AI compute power capacity
blurb: Meta is standing up a cloud business to rent out its excess AI compute, and the stock popped 8% on the news. The move lets Meta recoup part of the $145B it plans to spend on capex this year, easing investor unease about the buildout, but it also drops the company into a market already owned by Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and CoreWeave. It follows SpaceX, which is selling spare capacity into deals like Anthropic's $1.25B a month, a sign that even the biggest spenders now treat compute as a product, not just a cost.

title: OpenAI floats giving Trump administration 5 percent cut of AI boom
blurb: OpenAI has floated giving the US government a 5% ownership stake to ease tensions with the Trump administration and blunt the public backlash against AI, according to the Financial Times. Based on the company's $852B valuation, that cut would be worth roughly $42.6B, and the proposal reportedly assumes other US labs hand over similar stakes. The move fits a pattern of the administration taking equity for access, after a 10% stake in Intel and a demanded 15% cut of Nvidia and AMD's China chip sales. Regulatory protection, not just optics, is the trade OpenAI is buying.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic backer MGX raises one of the biggest AI funds ever as it closes at $49 billion
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX closed a $49B fund to back AI companies, one of the largest ever aimed at the sector and above its original $45B target. The money is chasing a market where AI startups have already raised a record $416.6B this year, nearly double all of 2025, with OpenAI and Anthropic taking most of it. MGX is a backer of both, having co-led OpenAI's $122B raise and Anthropic's $30B round, so the capital keeps concentrating at the top while the rest of the field competes for what's left.

title: Figma Grows 46% and Trades at 6x. Everyone Else Growing 30%+ Trades at 15-36x.
blurb: Figma is growing 46% with accelerating growth, 82% gross margins, 139% net dollar retention, and a 27% free cash flow margin, yet backing out its $1.6B of cash the stock trades around 6x forward revenue while peers growing 30% or more carry 15x to 36x. The gap exists because the market decided AI kills Figma once Anthropic shipped prompt-to-interface tooling. That is the only one of three reasons that would actually matter, and it is a bet on a thesis, not the numbers, which keep accelerating quarter over quarter.

title: Cloudflare sets a September 15 deadline for AI companies to differentiate their web crawlers into search, AI training, and AI agents or face being blocked (Samantha Elkins/NBC News)
blurb: Cloudflare is forcing AI companies to split their crawlers into three declared categories, search, AI training, and AI agents, by September 15 or get blocked at the network edge. The move gives publishers granular control to allow the bots that send traffic while refusing the ones that only harvest training data. If you run a crawler, undeclared or bundled bots become a liability overnight, and if you depend on scraped web data, the open pipe you have been drinking from is about to get metered.

title: Nvidia Offers Revenue Sharing Model for Aspiring AI Startups
blurb: Nvidia is now trading GPU access for equity-like upside, granting token credits to developers in exchange for a cut of their future revenue. The move lowers the upfront compute wall that has kept smaller teams off frontier hardware, but it also ties a startup's economics to its supplier for the life of the product. Nvidia already sits at the center of the AI stack as the dominant chip vendor, and revenue sharing extends that leverage from selling hardware into owning a slice of what gets built on it.

title: Anthropic Economic Index June 2026 Report
blurb: Claude usage has shifted from chat conversations to long-running agentic tasks, and Anthropic's latest Economic Index rebuilt its data pipeline to track it, sampling down to the hourly level and classifying the output of each session. The clearest signal is that compute now scales with value: the tokens an output consumes rise with the estimated worth of the work, so building a website pulls far more from Claude than translating a document. Usage also tracks the rhythms of real life, with work queries falling on weekends except in the highest-paid occupations.

title: OpenAI has reportedly offered Washington a 5% stake worth $42.6bn
blurb: OpenAI has reportedly offered the US government a 5% equity stake, worth roughly $42.6bn at its $852bn valuation, structured as donated shares routed through a public wealth fund rather than a cash sale. The move is voluntary and small by design, and OpenAI wants Anthropic, Google, and Meta to cede similar stakes through the same vehicle, though none has agreed. It also front-runs a far more aggressive alternative: Bernie Sanders' proposed 50% one-time stock tax on large AI companies. Both plans rest on the same premise, that the public should hold a claim on frontier AI before the value fully accrues to private shareholders.

title: OpenAI Proposes Giving 5% Stake to US Government: FT
blurb: OpenAI has opened preliminary talks about handing the US government a 5% stake, part of a broader proposal in which Washington would hold 5% of each leading American AI developer. The FT reports the idea would tie the frontier labs directly to the state that regulates them, subsidizes their power, and could become their largest customer. For founders, it signals that AI is drifting toward a quasi-national-champion model, where the biggest players carry government equity and everyone else competes against companies with Washington on the cap table.

title: Devin Fusion
blurb: Cognition released Devin Fusion, a multi-model coding harness that holds frontier and Fable 5-level performance at 35% lower cost on FrontierCode, its own benchmark for both code correctness and quality. The trick is a "sidekick" architecture: a frontier main agent handles the plan, the ambiguity calls, and the final review, then delegates the routine work to a cheaper parallel agent, with routing that shifts mid-session as difficulty changes. For teams burning cash running the priciest model on every task, the signal is that intelligent routing, not blanket frontier usage, is becoming the default way to control coding spend.

title: SoftBank Plans to Rent AI Compute in US at 10-Gigawatt Scale
blurb: SoftBank will start renting AI compute to US companies next fiscal year, targeting 10 gigawatts of capacity from its growing data-center pipeline. That puts it head-to-head with CoreWeave and Nebius in the neocloud market, backed by a balance sheet neither rival can match. The move signals that the scarce resource in AI is shifting from models to the power and silicon underneath them, and the players who control that capacity increasingly set the terms for everyone building on top.

title: Why jet engines aren't made in China
blurb: China has spent fifty years trying and failing to build jet engines at parity with the West, and the reason is instructive. Jet engines are a low-margin, slow-iteration market where reliability and manufacturing consistency decide everything, and every finished product faces an internationally enforced regulatory barrier. That combination neutralizes China's usual strengths in cheap capital, skilled labor, and speed-to-scale. The industries where state capitalism stalls reveal where the West's real comparative advantage still lives.

title: Amazon has enough satellites to launch its Starlink competitor
blurb: Amazon says its Leo network now has 396 satellites in low-Earth orbit, enough for continuous service across initial latitudes and on track for a mid-2026 commercial launch. That start is deliberately modest: SpaceX went live in 2020 with nearly 900 satellites and a narrow, glitchy beta before scaling to over 10,000 satellites and 160-plus countries. Amazon plans 3,232 Leo satellites total but is behind schedule, partly because Blue Origin's reusable New Glenn still isn't flying regularly. A second serious satellite-broadband provider is coming, but Starlink's lead is measured in years, not months.

title: Nvidia promises to financially backstop young cloud providers like Firmus that rent out its AI chips, in exchange for a revenue share through a new program (Phoebe Liu/The Information)
blurb: Nvidia is now using its balance sheet to guarantee revenue for the young cloud providers that buy its most expensive chips, taking a revenue share in return through a new program. The move keeps demand for GPUs high by de-risking the purchase for smaller renters like Firmus who could never carry that inventory alone. It also quietly makes Nvidia a financial stakeholder in its own customers, tightening a loop where the chips, the financing, and the eventual returns all route back through one company.

title: Anthropic is bringing back Claude Fable 5 globally after US lifts export control order — where can enterprises access it?
blurb: Anthropic restored global access to Claude Fable 5, its most powerful released model, after the U.S. Commerce Department withdrew the emergency export controls it imposed on June 12 that had forced the company to pull the model days after launch. Fable 5 is back across Claude.ai, the Claude Platform, Claude Code, and Cowork, with the hyperscalers being re-enabled. Its cybersecurity counterpart Mythos 5 stays behind a vetted-access wall for a set of US organizations, a reminder that frontier capability now moves through government approval, not just shipping speed.

title: Together AI raises 800 million dollars in Series C as open-source cloud demand surges
blurb: Together AI raised $800M at an $8B valuation, led by Aramco Ventures with Nvidia among the backers, roughly tripling the $3B mark it hit in its February 2025 Series B. The company now cites more than $1B in annual bookings and says open-source model usage on its platform has tripled over the past year, per OpenRouter data. The signal for founders is that demand for open-source AI cloud, the layer sitting between hyperscalers and developers wanting alternatives to OpenAI and Anthropic, is real enough to command hyperscaler-adjacent capital while GPU supply stays tight.

title: Google loses final appeal over $4.7 billion EU Android antitrust fine
blurb: Europe's highest court upheld a €4.1 billion ($4.67 billion) fine against Google for using Android to lock rivals out of search, ending a decade-long appeal that started in 2016. The ruling matters less for the check Google writes than for the precedent, since it confirms that pre-installation and default-placement deals count as abuse of dominance. Google still faces separate EU cases under the Digital Markets Act over self-preferencing in search and steering restrictions in the Play Store, so the operator lesson is that default-bundling as a distribution moat is now legally radioactive in Europe.

title: Finance and Tech Cut 28,000 Jobs Monthly as AI Takes Hold
blurb: Finance and tech are cutting a combined 28,000 jobs a month as AI starts absorbing routine work. The pattern isn't mass layoffs yet but slower hiring, higher attrition, and roles that simply go unfilled when someone leaves. These two sectors are the early signal because their work is the most exposed, and the quiet version of AI displacement, headcount that never gets backfilled, is harder to see and easier for management to justify than a public round of cuts.

title: Weave Robotics launches Isaac 1, a $7,999 home robot with Fall 2026 deliveries
blurb: Weave Robotics launched Isaac 1, a $7,999 home robot that folds laundry, resets rooms, and navigates autonomously with human teleoperators on standby to guarantee task completion. The teleoperation fallback is the tell: full-stack home robotics still can't run unattended, so the business model quietly depends on remote human labor priced into a consumer hardware tag. First shipments hit California in fall 2026, which makes this a preorder for a capability that isn't fully autonomous yet, sold at a price that assumes the autonomy gap closes over the air.

title: Bending Spoons defies SaaS slump, surges 40% on first day of trading
blurb: Bending Spoons closed its first trading day at $40.50, nearly 40% above its $29 IPO price, giving the Milan-based rollup a $25.7B market cap, more than double its last private valuation. The bet isn't AI-native software but the opposite: buying aging brands like AOL, Evernote, and Vimeo, then cutting costs and raising prices until they turn profitable. It worked, with Q1 revenue of $601M and $27.4M in net income against a $112M loss a year earlier, and public markets rewarded the acquire-fix-hold model even as pure SaaS multiples slumped.

title: Meta, like SpaceX, looks to turn excess AI compute into cash
blurb: Meta is building a cloud infrastructure business to sell access to its AI compute and models, putting it against AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure. The move follows SpaceX's xAI leasing out capacity weeks earlier, and it signals that the AI race may reward whoever owns the data centers rather than whoever ships the best models. Meta has committed $182.9 billion to AI infrastructure but hasn't shown real demand for Llama or Meta AI, so reselling raw compute CoreWeave-style is how it starts earning a return on that spend.

title: ChatGPT developer OpenAI said to discuss offering U.S. government a 5% stake: FT
blurb: OpenAI has discussed handing the U.S. government a 5% equity stake as part of a plan to share AI-generated wealth with the public and ease political scrutiny of the industry, per the FT. The proposal, modeled on Alaska's Permanent Fund, would ask leading labs to contribute equity to a public investment vehicle, though it is early, would likely need Congressional approval, and it is unclear whether Anthropic, Google, or Meta would follow. It also lands as OpenAI weighs an IPO, having filed draft paperwork with the SEC in June with advisers reportedly eyeing a 2027 listing.

title: Google Loses EU Top Court Fight Over €4.1 Billion Android Fine
blurb: Europe's top court upheld the €4.1 billion ($4.7 billion) antitrust fine against Google, ruling that regulators were right to penalize it for using Android's dominance to lock in its search and app businesses. The decision closes an eight-year fight and cements the EU's power to police how platform owners bundle their own services. For any founder building on top of a dominant OS or app store, the ruling signals that Brussels will keep forcing those gatekeepers to leave room, though the fines land years after the damage is done.

title: Ex-DeepMind researchers land record Creandum funding to scale AI agents for Nasdaq
blurb: Three ex-DeepMind researchers behind DeepStack raised a Series A at a $500M-plus valuation, led by Creandum in what the VC calls its largest-ever single investment. Prague-based EquiLibre runs reinforcement learning agents that already trade billions of dollars daily on the S&P 500 and Nasdaq through a quant partnership, and most of the new capital goes straight to compute. The bet is that trading is the purest testing ground for RL, no sales cycle or marketing to hide behind, and the founders now frame the open question as how big the approach scales rather than whether it works.

title: MGX Raises $49 Billion for AI Deals
blurb: MGX, a two-year-old Abu Dhabi firm, raised $49 billion for one of the largest funds ever dedicated to AI deals, vaulting it into the top tier of investors in the sector. Sovereign capital from the Gulf is now setting the price at the frontier, where a single fund can absorb rounds that used to require a syndicate. For founders raising at scale, the pool of capital large enough to lead an AI mega-round is concentrating in a handful of state-backed vehicles, and MGX just became one of the biggest.

title: French bid to block UK from €5bn EU fund sparks backlash
blurb: France is pushing to bar the UK from the EU's €5bn Scaleup Europe Fund, and founders and investors across the continent are pushing back. The fight is really about whether European capital pools stay narrowly national or open up to the region's deepest startup hub, which happens to sit outside the bloc. If protectionism wins, the fund risks starving Europe's scaleups of exactly the cross-border reach the money was meant to create.

title: Franklin Templeton Launches Franklin Crypto for Institutional Allocators
blurb: Franklin Templeton, which manages roughly $1.78 trillion, closed its acquisition of 250 Digital and switched on Franklin Crypto, an actively managed digital-asset arm aimed at pensions, sovereign wealth funds, and other institutional allocators. It now runs three institutional crypto lines: the BENJI on-chain money fund, tokenized treasuries that crossed $2.5 billion this year, and a pending pair of Bitcoin DRIP ETFs. Part of the deal was even paid in BENJI tokens, structuring the acquisition partly on chain. A $1.78 trillion manager building an active crypto desk signals that institutional allocation has moved from tokenized cash to taking real positions.

title: The European Court of Justice rules that Google's earlier defeat against a €4.1B European Commission penalty over abusing Android market power should stand (Bloomberg)
blurb: Google's €4.1B Android antitrust fine will stand after the European Court of Justice upheld the EU's earlier ruling that the company abused the operating system's market power. It closes off Google's last major avenue of appeal and locks in the penalty after roughly seven years of litigation. For any platform that bundles services or leans on default placement to steer users, the EU has now confirmed it will treat that leverage as an antitrust liability, not just a business model.

title: Data Center Firm Switch Seeks $2 Billion in Funding Round
blurb: Switch is raising about $2 billion in a private round led by Andreessen Horowitz, according to people familiar with the deal. The capital is flowing into data center capacity, the physical layer underneath the AI boom, rather than into another model or application startup. As frontier labs and hyperscalers compete for compute, the infrastructure providers that own the power, land, and buildings are becoming some of the largest venture bets in the market.

title: QUANTUM SYSTEMS lève 1,2 milliard de dollars : la bataille des drones est terminée, celle des plateformes de défense commence
blurb: Quantum Systems raised $1.2B in a Series D at close to an $8B valuation, and it is repositioning from drone manufacturer to defense software platform, a European answer to Anduril. The bet is that airframes are becoming commodities and the real moat is MOSAIC UXS, the interoperability layer that fuses drones, robots, sensors, and satellites into one command architecture. Wall Street is buying the thesis, with Blackstone, Advent, Fidelity, and Wellington all in the round, signaling that European defense is now being priced as strategic infrastructure rather than hardware.

title: Together AI raises $800M at $8.3B valuation as enterprises ditch closed models for open-source
blurb: Together AI raised $800M at an $8.3B valuation, led by Aramco Ventures, more than doubling its $3.3B mark from February 2025. The company never built a foundation model. It built the cloud that runs open-source ones like DeepSeek and Kimi, and it claims customers cut inference costs six to 60 times versus closed-model rivals while matching quality. Annual bookings have passed $1.15B as enterprises decide frontier-lab pricing isn't worth it, with Cursor and Cognition already on the platform.

title: OpenAI reportedly wants all AI companies to give the US government a stake in their businesses
blurb: OpenAI's Sam Altman has reportedly proposed that leading AI labs, including Google, Anthropic, xAI, and Meta, each hand the US government a 5% equity stake to share the AI boom's upside with the public. The play is political: labs are already hitting release roadblocks, with the administration forcing GPT-5.6 into a limited government-approved preview and briefly ordering Anthropic to pull its Mythos and Fable models. Government ownership recently smoothed things for Intel, where a 10% stake turned an antagonist into a partner, but any AI deal is early-stage and would need Congressional approval.

title: Musk-Altman Judge Enlists Mediator to Resolve AI Titans’ Battle
blurb: The judge in the Musk-Altman lawsuit has brought in a mediator to try to settle the dispute between the two founders and their competing AI companies. The move signals the court would rather see the fight resolved privately than dragged through a public trial that could expose OpenAI's governance and the terms of its for-profit conversion. For founders, the case is a reminder that a co-founder split at the top can follow the company for years, and the cleaner the original ownership and control terms, the less there is to litigate later.

title: Rocket Lab Enters the Satcom Market With $8B Iridium Deal
blurb: Rocket Lab announced an $8B cash-and-stock deal to acquire Iridium, buying its way past launch and manufacturing into satcom services. The bet isn't consumer internet, where Starlink and Amazon Leo dominate, but Iridium's globally licensed L-band spectrum, the low-frequency band that penetrates weather and underpins emergency services, GPS, and aviation. The acquisition more than doubles Rocket Lab's revenue, adding Iridium's ~$872M against its own $602M FY25, plus the free cash flow to fund new direct-to-device and alternative-PNT services. Vertical integration is becoming the space playbook, and owning defensible spectrum is the moat launch capacity alone can't buy.

title: NASA Praises Blue Origin’s Mishap Progress, Announces Moon Base Contracts
blurb: NASA awarded roughly $600M in new CLPS lunar contracts, splitting the work across Astrobotic, Intuitive Machines, and Firefly to push toward monthly Moon landings with agency payloads. The vendors will fly updated versions of already-flown lander designs rather than new hardware, a bet on iteration over reinvention. Blue Origin, still recovering from last month's New Glenn explosion, holds several Moon contracts and remains in contention for Artemis III as early as 2027, but NASA said it is weighing other options while it studies Blue's recovery timeline.

title: Wayve undertakes $85M employee tender offer to “retain talent”
blurb: Employee tender offers are becoming the default liquidity path while the IPO window stays shut. Wayve, the UK autonomous-driving startup valued at $8.6B after its $1.2B Series D in February, is running an $85M tender that lets its 1,200 employees sell shares to new and existing investors, its second such offer since 2024. Stripe, ElevenLabs, and Revolut have done the same, and the pattern signals that private companies now treat structured secondaries, not the public markets, as the mechanism for rewarding and retaining early talent.

title: CMA consults on new requirements for Apple and Google’s mobile platforms
blurb: The UK's CMA is consulting on rules that would force Apple and Google to let app developers steer customers to off-platform payment, bypassing the mandatory 15-30% store fees, and to open NFC access for third-party iOS apps. The regulator expects any steering fees to be lower than current store charges, with savings passed to developers or reinvested. For UK founders, this cracks two of mobile's tightest chokepoints at once: the payment tax on every in-app transaction and the contactless rail that fintechs have been locked out of.

title: Drone maker Quantum Systems raises $1.2bn, eyes humanoids
blurb: Munich's Quantum Systems raised $1.2bn at an $8bn valuation, co-led by Blackstone, Airbus, Noteus, and Advent, and now says it has the firepower to consolidate a crowded European defence-tech market. The autonomous drone maker is expanding from air and land into maritime and larger aircraft, and cofounder Florian Seibel is teasing humanoids and a possible merger with strike-drone startup Stark. The round deliberately courted US and Asian investors to build what the company calls Europe's international neoprime, while an IPO readiness review keeps a 2027 listing on the table without committing to it.

title: Germany-based Quantum Systems, which sells surveillance drones used by Ukraine, raised $1.2B led by Blackstone, Noteus, Airbus, and Advent at an ~$8B valuation (Yazhou Sun/Bloomberg)
blurb: Quantum Systems, the German maker of surveillance drones used by Ukraine, raised $1.2B at roughly an $8B valuation, led by Blackstone, Noteus, Airbus, and Advent. The round more than doubled the company's valuation, and its investor list now pairs traditional venture money with defense primes and buyout giants. European defense tech has become one of the few categories drawing this scale of capital, as the war in Ukraine turns battlefield-proven hardware into a fundable asset class.

title: EU offers quota deal to end UK-France standoff over €5bn Scaleup fund
blurb: Brussels put a quota compromise on the table to break a UK-France deadlock over a €5bn Scaleup fund meant to back Europe's late-stage startups. The fight is over who gets access: France has pushed to limit how much non-EU capital and non-EU companies can tap the pool, while the UK wants in. The outcome sets the terms for where Europe's biggest growth-stage checks can flow, and for founders it decides whether the continent's scaleup money stays walled off by nationality or opens up.

title: SpaceX Analyst Debut Set to Test $2.2 Trillion Valuation
blurb: SpaceX begins Wall Street analyst coverage this week, the first real test of the $2.2 trillion valuation it carried into last month's record IPO. Investors have been flying largely blind since then, with few financial projections to anchor what the stock is actually worth. The debut forces the question every private-market believer has deferred for years: whether Starlink revenue and launch cadence justify a number bigger than any listed company except a handful of megacaps.

title: How Lime went from pandemic write-off to a $167M IPO
blurb: Lime priced its Nasdaq IPO at $25 a share on June 30, raising $167 million, six years after its valuation cratered from $2.4 billion in 2019 to about $510 million during the pandemic. The turnaround ran through Uber, which led a 2020 rescue investment, folded Lime's scooters and bikes into its app, and now plans to buy up to $20 million of shares in the offering. The lesson for founders is that distribution from a larger partner, not fresh capital alone, is what pulled a written-off micromobility bet back to public markets.

title: Mark Zuckerberg wants Meta to launch its own prediction market
blurb: Meta is building a standalone Polymarket-style prediction app, internally called "Arena," that Zuckerberg has greenlit as experimental but a top priority. The first version is points-based rather than real-money, more video game than betting platform, with cash possibly added later and Meta's social apps funneling users in. Prediction markets have pulled tens of billions in trading volume across Polymarket and Kalshi and drawn matching legal heat, from insider-trading cases to states suing over gambling laws. Meta is moving into a category that is growing fast and getting regulated in real time.

title: Higgsfield targets $5B valuation and $500M run rate as Sora folds and Runway retreats
blurb: Higgsfield is in talks to raise $300M to $500M at a $5B valuation, more than four times what the AI video startup was worth in January. The number is backed by a run rate the company says hit $500M in June, up from $200M at the end of 2025, with roughly 70% of it recurring enterprise revenue. The timing helps too, as Sora shut down in April and Runway pivoted to world models, leaving Higgsfield a clearer lane in a category two of its biggest rivals just vacated.

title: How AI Mentions and Cites Your Brand
blurb: AI names your brand far more often than it cites your site. A study of 12,000 AI responses across Google's AI products and GPT-5.4-mini found that only 23.1% of brand mentions came with a citation, while 69.9% of citations also named the brand. The takeaway for operators: track mentions and citations as separate visibility signals, because a mention with no citation reflects training-data memory while a live citation means the model went looking for evidence, and each is earned a different way.

title: CarbonSix, which is developing AI automation and robotic intelligence tech for manufacturing, raised a $40M Series A co-led by DSC Investment and LB Investment (FinSMEs)
blurb: CarbonSix raised a $40M Series A co-led by DSC Investment and LB Investment to build AI automation and robotic intelligence for manufacturing. The San Francisco physical-AI company is targeting the factory floor, where robots still handle a fraction of the tasks that could be automated. Capital is flowing toward companies that put AI to work in the physical world, not just in software, as investors bet the next wave of automation happens on the production line rather than the screen.

title: Congo Plans First Stock Market as AI Mineral Boom Draws Interest
blurb: The Democratic Republic of Congo is drawing up plans for its first stock exchange, aiming to channel investment into an economy riding surging demand for the minerals that feed the AI buildout. The country holds much of the world's cobalt and other inputs critical to data-center hardware, and rising prices are giving it leverage to formalize markets that were previously offshore or informal. For founders, it is a reminder that the AI boom is redrawing capital flows far below the model layer, into the raw materials and the countries that control them.

title: Alibaba-affiliate Ant Group rushes into humanoid robots with a dozen deals in 18 months
blurb: Ant Group has backed 12 humanoid robotics companies since the start of 2025, most recently leading a 500M yuan ($74M) round in Zeroth. The Alibaba affiliate is stitching together a full stack, from robot makers like Unitree and Galaxea to parts and software startups, and folding it into an AI-friendly version of Alipay. The bet is already showing demand, with Zeroth claiming 30,000 unit orders and 600% revenue growth as it preps North America and Europe sales this fall, right as Nvidia staffs up robotics teams across China.

title: Takeda, Insilico Strike AI Drug-Discovery Deal Worth Up to $600 Million
blurb: Takeda agreed to pay Insilico Medicine up to $600M to run drug candidates through its Pharma.AI platform across several therapeutic areas. The deal is another sign that big pharma is treating AI-designed molecules as a core part of the pipeline rather than a side experiment, paying real money for discovery speed instead of building the capability in-house. For AI-bio startups, it signals that the buyers are established drugmakers writing large milestone-based checks, and the moat is a proprietary platform with candidates far enough along to license.

title: 가트너 “에이전틱 AI에 SaaS 시장 재편…기존 업체는 위기, 서비스 기업은 기회”
blurb: Gartner projects that up to $234B in enterprise application spending, roughly 20% of all SaaS spend by 2030, will be exposed to "agentic arbitrage," where AI agents work across systems and users stop touching the underlying software interface. That breaks the link between seat growth and revenue that most SaaS vendors are built on, since a hidden UI stops being a differentiator. The winners are service and platform players that deliver measurable outcomes and hold onto customer context, while incumbents relying on dashboards and per-user pricing lose ground.

title: What Meta, Oracle moves say about data center economics
blurb: Hyperscalers are shifting from racing to build AI capacity to managing the financial risk of it. Oracle's latest 10-K spells out how its data center bets, including the $300B Stargate contract with OpenAI, could run over budget or fall through, while Meta is reportedly planning a new business to sell excess compute and recoup some of its spend. With the top four hyperscalers on track to spend up to $725B on AI infrastructure this year, the game is turning from land grab to return on infrastructure.

title: EchoStar’s satellite TV and wireless subsidiaries file for bankruptcy
blurb: EchoStar's DISH DBS and DISH Wireless subsidiaries filed for Chapter 11, using a prepackaged plan to repay debt early after selling more than $40B in spectrum to SpaceX and AT&T. The trigger was mundane: delays closing the AT&T sale left DISH DBS unable to cover $2B in debt due July 1. Creditors holding most DISH DBS notes and over $8.8B of Wireless debt already back the plan, and the units expect to emerge before October, with DISH TV, Sling, Boost Mobile, and Hughes untouched.

title: Competition regulator ‘falls short’ in app developer consultation
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority opened a consultation that could force Apple and Google to let developers steer users to payment options outside their app stores, sidestepping the 30% cut the two collect on 95% of UK mobile. The Coalition for App Fairness, whose members include Epic and Spotify, called the measure cosmetic because it stops short of barring fees on transactions the platforms never process. For any founder whose economics run through an app store, the lesson is that Strategic Market Status designation is proving easier to win than the conduct rules that would actually move margins.

title: Tapestry VC launches £60m fund to back repeat founders
blurb: Tapestry VC raised $80m for its Fund III, nearly three times its prior size, co-anchored by the British Business Bank alongside returning backers Railpen and Molten Ventures. The seed and pre-seed firm is doubling down on repeat founders across Europe and North America, the segment where the data is lopsided: 60% of Europe's 477 unicorns had a repeat founder, and those teams raised 45% more capital than first-timers. The bet is that expensive experience is the scarcest input in early-stage building, and it now commands its own dedicated capital.

title: Former Goldman Analyst’s AI Startup Is Said to Raise $22 Million
blurb: LinqAlpha, an AI investment-research startup founded by a former Goldman Sachs analyst, is close to raising $22M in a Series A. The round lands as AI tools push into the equity-research workflows that once defined junior banking and buy-side jobs, automating the note-reading, filing-parsing, and synthesis work analysts do by hand. The bet is that firms will pay to compress that research cycle rather than staff it, and the capital says investors expect the workflow to move that way.

title: Quantum Systems More Than Doubles Valuation to $8 Billion
blurb: German drone startup Quantum Systems raised $1.2B, more than doubling its post-money valuation to about $8B. The round is the latest sign that defense-related bets are pulling in capital at a pace that used to be reserved for consumer AI. European defense tech has become one of the few sectors where valuations are compounding this fast, as governments rearm and investors chase the buildout.

title: Airwallex hits $11B valuation as fintech pushes into AI-run finance
blurb: Airwallex raised $320M in a Series H at an $11B valuation, up 38% in six months, and is pushing into AI-run corporate finance and agentic payments. The pitch is that its decade of regulatory groundwork pays off here: more than 85 licenses across four regions, annualized revenue up 74% to $1.3B, and over 90% of that revenue from multi-product customers. The bet is that the same settlement rails and licenses that powered cross-border payments become the infrastructure layer for the agentic economy, though CEO Jack Zhang says AI spend has made margins too volatile to go public.

title: Nvidia competitor Etched hits $5B valuation, $1B in sales for AI chip
blurb: Etched, a specialized AI inference chip startup, has booked $1B in contract orders and raised $800M to date, including an unannounced $500M round in December at a $5B valuation. The bet is narrow: custom systems it calls frontier inference clusters, built to run inference faster, cheaper, and with better power efficiency than Nvidia GPUs, exactly the cost center every AI company is trying to shrink. In 2023 the founders couldn't get a single major investor to bite on the idea that AI would need specialized chips. Every one of them passed.

title: Tech leads first half stock gains — but the biggest winners weren't in the U.S.
blurb: Tech led the market in the first half of 2026, but the gains that mattered were offshore. MSCI's emerging-markets tech index rose more than 90% and the European version added 44.8%, while the U.S. index topped by Nvidia, Apple, and Microsoft managed just 19.4%. The split runs deepest in semiconductors, where SK Hynix soared roughly 300%, TSMC gained 55.5%, and ASML added 86.8%, even as Microsoft shed 22.9%. If your read on the AI trade still centers on the Mag 7, the alpha this year sat in Seoul, Taipei, and Amsterdam.

title: Quantum Systems lands €1 billion Series D in one of Europe’s largest DefenceTech financings
blurb: Quantum Systems raised €1 billion ($1.2 billion) in a Series D co-led by Blackstone, Airbus, Advent, and Noteus, more than doubling its valuation to around €7 billion. It is one of Europe's largest DefenceTech rounds, and the company says it is already profitable with triple-digit growth and deployments worldwide. The capital signals where European defence money is flowing: interoperable, AI-powered autonomous systems tied together by software, with Quantum's MOSAIC UXS backbone as the bet that the next prime is defined by the network connecting drones, sensors, and counter-drone systems rather than any single platform.

title: Build raises £6.4m to accelerate infrastructure projects with AI
blurb: Build raised $8.5M in seed funding led by Index Ventures to automate infrastructure workflows like site selection and due diligence with AI. The pitch is that demand for AI infrastructure, power, and industrial capacity is outpacing supply, while the consulting and engineering firms delivering these projects lack the margins to invest in AI themselves. The signal is in the cap table, with OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar, Blackstone CTO John Stecher, and senior figures from OpenAI, Meta AI Research, and Google Maps backing the bet that slow, fragmented workflows are the real constraint on building physical infrastructure.

title: Deutschlands nächstes Mega-Unicorn: Drohnen-Startup Quantum Systems sammelt 1,2 Milliarden ein
blurb: Munich defense-tech startup Quantum Systems raised $1.2B at a roughly $8B valuation, one of Europe's largest funding rounds in the sector and an eightfold jump in a year. Founded in 2015 to build agricultural drones, it pivoted almost entirely to military use after Russia's invasion of Ukraine, and now sells autonomous air, ground, and counter-drone systems tied together by its MOSAIC UXS software. The round, led by Blackstone, Advent, and Airbus, funds international expansion, and unlike most fast-growing defense startups Quantum is already profitable, targeting roughly €700M in 2026 revenue.

title: TwelveLabs raises $100M as Amazon secures AWS as its preferred cloud
blurb: TwelveLabs raised $100M to index existing video, not generate it, naming AWS its preferred cloud as Amazon joined the round.

title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers found real-world RSA keys with regularly spaced blocks of all zeros in their moduli, a sparsity pattern that makes them far easier to factor, surfaced by scanning Certificate Transparency logs, TLS/SSH scans, and PGP dumps with the open-source badkeys project. The flaw traces to independent implementations failing the same way, including CompleteFTP's key generation and certificates once issued to Yahoo and Verizon, suggesting cryptanalytic attacks tuned for this failure class are worth building.

title: The <Usermedia> HTML Element
blurb: Chrome 151 ships the `<usermedia>` HTML element, a declarative, user-activated control for camera and microphone access that hands back a MediaStream directly and drops the boilerplate of script-triggered `getUserMedia()` prompts. It follows `<geolocation>` in the Capability Elements suite, and Origin Trial data showed the recovery path helps users who first denied access, with Google Meet reporting a 131% jump in successful permission recovery.

title: Senior SWE-Bench: open-source benchmark that assesses agents as senior engineers
blurb: Senior SWE-Bench is an open-source benchmark that grades coding agents like senior engineers, scoring them on under-specified feature tasks, runtime-investigation bug fixes, and adherence to unstated codebase conventions rather than pass/fail unit tests. Claude Opus 4.8 tops the leaderboard at just 24%, with every frontier model failing more than 75% of tasks on correctness and taste.

title: Safari’s new MCP server lets coding agents inspect and debug websites
blurb: Safari Technology Preview 247 ships the Safari MCP server, which connects coding agents to a live Safari window so they can inspect the DOM, read console logs and network requests, capture screenshots, and interact with page elements. It collapses the browser-prompt-agent debugging loop into a single tool call, giving agents the rendered-page context they previously only got from a developer manually pasting screenshots or logs.

title: Text AI watermarks will always be trivial to remove
blurb: The author argues that text AI watermarks will always be trivial to strip, because text is too compressed a medium to hide a signature without degrading output quality, unlike images. This matters because the EU AI Act's Article 50 requires all AI output to be detectable as artificially generated by August 2026, a mandate the post contends is technically unenforceable through watermarking alone.

title: Asahi Linux 7.1 Progress Report
blurb: The Asahi Linux 7.1 report covers M3 support progress and the fix for macOS 27's Golden Gate beta silently hiding the Asahi boot option, which turned out to be an APFS volume flag that Apple's boot tooling had ignored until this release. New installs now set the bootable flag automatically, and existing users on the beta can restore access by re-running the installer's "Fix" mode.

title: TypeScript 7.0 reaches release candidate stage
blurb: Microsoft released a release candidate for TypeScript 7.0, a rewrite of the compiler in Go that runs about 10x faster than the JavaScript-based 6.0 by using native code speed and shared-memory parallelism. Parsing, type checking, and emitting now run in parallel, and a `@typescript/typescript6` compatibility package ships a `tsc6` executable so 7.0 can install side-by-side with 6.0 until the stable API lands in 7.1.

title: Miles: A PyTorch-Native Stack for Large-Scale LLM RL Post-Training
blurb: RadixArk released Miles, an open source PyTorch-native framework for large-scale LLM reinforcement learning post-training that composes SGLang for rollout, Megatron-LM for training, and Ray for orchestration behind a deliberately small, pluggable trainer. It ships MoE-aware rollout/training alignment, unified low-precision recipes, fast NCCL/RDMA weight synchronization, and built-in observability and fault tolerance, letting teams swap reward, loss, and rollout logic through Python modules without forking the core.

title: Beyond Document Grounding: Span-Level Hallucination Detection over Code, Tool Output, and Documents
blurb: This paper introduces a benchmark for span-level hallucination detection over source code, developer-tool output, and structured documents, extending grounding checks beyond the natural-language RAG datasets they are usually measured on. The authors' fine-tuned Qwen3.5-2B detector hits 0.689 span-F1 on the unified test set and 0.60 on code-agent output, beating LettuceDetect-large at 0.17 and the best zero-shot LLM judges at 0.22.

title: NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project Pushes Weakened TLS Post-Quantum Standard
blurb: The author argues that NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project is steering the IETF toward "ietf-tls-mlkem," a post-quantum TLS standard weaker than the hybrid "ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem" alternative, swapping honest "NSA wants this" arguments for fake technical ones. After losing the working-group vote, NSA called another and is packing the room, and the post asks readers to email the TLS mailing list opposing draft-ietf-tls-mlkem-08 before 7 July 2026.

title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
blurb: MCP's 2026-07-28 revision makes the protocol stateless at the layer, closing off session hijacking and unsolicited server prompts while tightening authentication, with legacy versions deprecated over a 12-month window. Akamai's analysis warns the rewrite shifts risk onto implementations: predictable tracking IDs and state objects open the door to cross-tenant workflow hijacking, and new MCP HTTP headers introduce desync attacks and secret leakage if developers map API keys or PII into them.

title: Are Performance-Optimization Benchmarks Reliably Measuring Coding Agents?
blurb: This paper audits three repository-level performance-optimization benchmarks (GSO, SWE-Perf, SWE-fficiency) and finds their leaderboard scores conflate runtime instability, scoring-rule quirks, and task saturation, with reference patches passing cross-machine replay for only 39/102 GSO and 11/140 SWE-Perf tasks. The authors show public rankings flip on 9 of 28 pairwise comparisons depending on the scoring rule, arguing aggregate scores overstate coding-agent progress and hide the real per-task performance gaps.

title: New ChocoPoC RAT Targets Vulnerability Researchers via Fake PoC Exploit Repos
blurb: Malware researchers YesWeHack and Sekoia disclosed ChocoPoC, a remote access trojan hidden in fake GitHub proof-of-concept repos that target vulnerability researchers racing to test fresh CVEs. The visible PoC looks clean while the payload hides in a transitively pulled Python dependency that only detonates when it detects a real PoC file, letting it slip past quick code review and bare sandboxes while it steals browser credentials, cookies, and files and opens a shell.

title: Kimi K2.7 Code is generally available in GitHub Copilot
blurb: GitHub Copilot added Kimi K2.7 Code, the first open-weight model selectable in the Copilot model picker, hosted by GitHub on Azure and billed at provider list pricing under usage-based billing. It is rolling out to Pro, Pro+, and Max plans in VS Code and other IDEs now, while Business and Enterprise admins must enable the model policy before their teams can select it.

title: Introducing Flink's Native S3 FileSystem: Built for Performance, Designed for Production
blurb: Apache Flink 2.3 introduces flink-s3-fs-native, a Hadoop-free S3 filesystem built specifically for Flink that replaces the two existing Hadoop-based plugins on the s3:// scheme. The native plugin supports RecoverableWriter for exactly-once sinks while keeping a fast read path, drops the hadoop-common dependency tree and AWS SDK v1, and ships as an experimental opt-in already running in production at scale.

title: How we used DSPy to turn AI evaluations into better responses in Dash chat
blurb: Dropbox used DSPy to build a two-stage feedback loop for its Dash chat agent, first calibrating LLM-as-judge evaluations against human-labeled examples, then using those tuned judges to optimize the agent's system prompt. The post walks through the judge-calibration setup and DSPy optimizers like GEPA and MIPROv2, with the result being significantly fewer incomplete answers and lower token usage without degrading answer quality.

title: Opening up 'Zero-Knowledge Proof' technology to promote privacy in age assurance
blurb: Google open sourced its Zero-Knowledge Proof libraries (longfellow-zk) to support EU age assurance, letting users prove a claim like being over 18 without disclosing any other data. The release targets the eIDAS regulation taking effect in 2026, giving developers a ready cryptographic implementation to build into the European Digital Identity Wallet and other privacy-focused ID systems.

title: Longinus: 2 Boundaries in One Bug, Piercing Chrome's Renderer and V8 Sandbox with a Single Vulnerability, CVE-2026-6307
blurb: The Longinus writeup dissects CVE-2026-6307, a single Chrome vulnerability that breaches two security boundaries at once, escaping both the V8 sandbox and the renderer process. The author walks through how one memory bug lets an attacker pivot across layers normally treated as independent defenses, collapsing the multi-stage exploit chain typically needed to reach the same depth.

title: SWE-Doctor: Guiding Software Engineering Agents with Runtime Diagnosis from Multi-Faceted Bug Reproduction Tests
blurb: SWE-Doctor is a software issue resolution agent that guides patch generation with runtime diagnoses built from multi-faceted bug reproduction tests, addressing the finding that fail-to-pass BRTs often cover only one manifestation of an issue and yield partial patches. It generates BRTs for each behavioral requirement, debugs them into diagnosis records, and reaches 75.7% resolution on SWE-bench Verified and 59.4% on SWE-bench Pro, beating baselines by 8 points on Pro.

title: Run any Dockerfile on Vercel
blurb: Vercel now deploys any container by adding a `Dockerfile.vercel` to your project, building the image and running it on Fluid compute with autoscaling and no local daemon, registry, or cluster to manage. Any HTTP stack works as long as the server listens on `$PORT`, and active CPU pricing bills only for the time your code actually runs, scaling instances down to zero when traffic stops.

title: Knowledge-Enhanced Agentic Vulnerability Repair
blurb: KeaRepair is an agentic automated vulnerability repair system that grounds patch generation in verified program facts and knowledge mined from historical vulnerability-patch pairs, using a ReAct-style agent for diagnosis and a closed loop of compilation, PoC replay, and test-suite runs to refine fixes. Paired with Gemini-3.1-Pro, it repaired 46 of 55 reproducible C/C++ vulnerabilities, an 83.64% rate, and fixed six that no baseline could address.

title: Granular Usage Attribution for dbt Pipelines with Query Tags
blurb: Databricks' Query Tags, now in Public Preview, auto-inject metadata like dbt_model_name into every query a dbt pipeline runs and support custom tags for team, cost center, and environment, all recorded in system.query.history as a queryable MAP. The dbt-databricks adapter (1.11+) applies tags at profile, project, and model levels, turning per-model cost attribution and performance debugging into a single SQL query with zero configuration for the auto-tags.

title: A peek into Reddit's anti-spam internals
blurb: A researcher captured screenshots of Reddit's live spam-removal internals in 2021, after a bug briefly swapped the generic "Removed: Auto" text for the full removal reason on their moderation feed. The leaked strings expose the signals Reddit's classifier weighs per action, including a Perspective API spam score, account age, karma, report count, email domain, network org, and a TLS fingerprint.

title: The MCP Release Candidate Survival Guide: Apps, Auth, Deprecations, and Tool Schemas
blurb: The MCP 2026-07-28 release candidate, the largest spec revision since launch, makes the protocol stateless at the transport layer, so servers can no longer hide required state in the connection and each request carries its version, capabilities, and method in headers and _meta. The guide walks through migrating Streamable HTTP transports, replaying server-initiated flows via InputRequiredResult, and treating MCP Apps as real UI surfaces before the final spec locks.

title: Where Severity Scores Go Wrong: “Just Add Prototype Pollution”
blurb: JFrog's security team argues that CVSS severity scores routinely overstate real-world risk, citing their 2025 finding that 96% of NVD critical-severity CVEs and 65% of high-severity ones warranted downgrades once exploitation requirements and environmental constraints were factored in. Using JavaScript prototype pollution as the case study, the post shows how vulnerabilities with identical actual impact draw wildly different scores, making the raw number a poor trigger for patching priority.

title: RepoRescue: An Empirical Study of LLM Agents on Whole-Repository Compatibility Rescue
blurb: RepoRescue benchmarks whether LLM agents can restore old repositories that passed historically but break after dependency and runtime drift, using 193 Python and 122 Java repos that each fail only after modernization. Under runtime blocking that stops agents editing failing tests, Kimi rescues 41.5% of repos and the union of systems hits 62.7%, with cross-file coordination the main failure mode and passing test suites proving only a weak signal of real fixes.

title: ZCode – Harness for GLM-5.2
blurb: ZCode is an agentic coding harness optimized for Z.ai's GLM-5.2, spanning planning, code, review, and deploy across 20+ integrated tools. It supports 20+ coding tools and lets developers start or steer sessions from WeChat, Feishu, or Telegram, with a Goals feature for continuous planning, execution, and verification on longer-running work.

title: 14x faster embeddings: how we rebuilt the ONNX path in Manticore
blurb: Manticore Search 27.1.5 replaces its SentenceTransformers/Candle embedding path with an ONNX Runtime backend that runs ~14x faster on the same hardware, model, and weights, lifting throughput from 5-11 docs/sec to 70-230 docs/sec across the full thread and batch grid. The engineering log covers the two changes that mattered most, turning off intra-op spinning and parallelizing inside each call so a single client thread with batch sizes of 32-128 maximizes ingest.

title: Qwen 3.6 27B is the sweet spot for local development
blurb: The author argues Qwen 3.6 27B is the first local model practical enough to serve as a general-purpose coding assistant, citing a hexagonal minesweeper it built correctly from a single prompt with proper pnpm packaging. The dense 27B variant runs locally via llama.cpp at Q8_0 quantization and outperforms the faster 35B A3B mixture-of-experts version on instruction-following, at the cost of speed and a hot machine.

title: AI coding tools are speeding up code, not delivery
blurb: The author argues that AI coding assistants speed up how fast developers produce code but leave end-to-end delivery times largely unchanged, since review, testing, integration, and deployment remain the real bottlenecks. Faster code generation shifts the constraint downstream rather than removing it, so teams that measure success by output volume will miss where their cycle time actually goes.

title: Nano Banana 2 Lite
blurb: Google released Nano Banana 2 Lite, also known as Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite Image, positioned as its fastest and cheapest image model built for speed and scale. Simon Willison's test on a Where's Waldo-style prompt beat his April results from earlier Nano Banana models, though the output still misspelled "Forest Festival" two different ways in the same image.

title: Why an idempotency key isn’t an idempotency guarantee
blurb: The author traces a double-charge bug to a payment retry that fired after a 2-second client timeout, even though the provider had already charged the card and just answered slowly. The post's argument is that a timeout is a third outcome, neither success nor failure, so an idempotency key only prevents duplicates when the provider actually honors it across retries.

title: Fixing a kubelet memory leak in Kubernetes 1.36
blurb: The author traced a kubelet memory leak in Kubernetes 1.36 to unbounded context cancellation, surfaced first on a cheap 2 GiB DigitalOcean node where memory pressure exposed it faster than an abundant one would. The post walks through capturing a live heap profile via pprof over the kubelet debug endpoint and reading `go tool pprof -top` output, which pinned most in-use objects on `context.cancelCtx.propagateCancel`.

title: GitHub’s new tool helps prevent costly open-source license violations
blurb: GitHub launched License Compliance in public preview, a GitHub Advanced Security feature that scans dependencies added through pull requests and flags those whose licenses violate an organization's policy. It runs in an Evaluate mode that annotates PRs without blocking merges, letting teams approve licenses or grant package-specific exceptions before noncompliant dependencies reach production and become expensive to rip out later.

title: Godot will no longer accept AI-authored code contributions
blurb: The Godot Foundation will amend its contribution guidelines to ban AI-authored code, PRs submitted by AI agents, and AI-generated text in human communication, arguing that "AI cannot take responsibility, and we can't trust heavy users of AI to understand their code enough to fix it." The maintainers frame it as a mentorship problem as much as a quality one, since review time spent correcting slop PRs no longer develops the future maintainers the project depends on.

title: We Ran 250 AI Agent Evals to Find Out if Skills Beat Docs. The Answer Is More Complicated Than We Expected
blurb: Wix ran 250 controlled evals comparing AI agents on standard docs, AI-optimized docs, and purpose-built skills, and found that purpose-built skills only win when kept current. A slightly stale skill underperforms the docs it was derived from, since hand-maintained skills drift the moment an API adds a required field or deprecates a method, making well-structured documentation often the better "skill.

title: “It's Hard to Eval” Is a Product Smell
blurb: The author argues that "our product is hard to eval" is a product smell, since artifacts that are hard for you to verify are usually hard for users too, often forcing them to redo the work to check the output. Drawing on three AI products he advised, he shows that designing for verifiability, like exposing checkable artifacts and the underlying SQL in a data agent, should come before building evals.

title: X now offers an MCP server to make its platform easier for AI tools to use
blurb: X released a hosted Model Context Protocol server that lets MCP-compatible tools like Claude and Cursor hit the X API using a user's own account permissions, removing the need to build and host a custom server. It exposes existing read capabilities such as search, post lookup, and trend analysis but blocks Write endpoints, so tools can retrieve and analyze X data without posting autonomously.

title: Cost Attribution in Discord's API
blurb: Discord extended its application profiling tooling to attribute hosting costs by product feature across a unified Python codebase of 1,700+ API endpoints running the same code in hundreds of Kubernetes deployments. Since cloud billing only splits costs by deployment and each worker juggles many features concurrently, the team allocates spend by measuring how much CPU time each deployment spends in feature-related code paths.

title: Rise From The Ashes: LLM-based Static Analysis for Deep Learning Framework Bugs
blurb: Phoenix is the first LLM-based static analysis tool for deep learning frameworks, modeling cross-language tensor flows as a structured semantic bridge intermediate representation that a multi-agent workflow analyzes for bugs without runtime execution. It has found 31 real new bugs in PyTorch across Intel CPU, NVIDIA CUDA, and Apple MPS backends, with 20 bug-fixing patches already merged upstream.

title: From Registry to Repository: How AI Agent Skills Are Written, Adapted, and Maintained
blurb: An empirical study of 18,463 AI agent skills finds reuse is mostly one-time copying, with 53% never modified after adoption.

title: Apache Flink 2.3.0 Release Announcement
blurb: Apache Flink 2.3.0 expands Flink SQL with new FROM_CHANGELOG and TO_CHANGELOG process table functions that convert between append-only and dynamic changelog tables, plus a redesigned native S3 filesystem built on AWS SDK v2. The release implements 15 FLIPs, adds application-level lifecycle management for production deployments, and reworks SinkUpsertMaterializer for better performance on some SQL workloads.

title: Hanami 3.0: In Full Bloom
blurb: Hanami 3.0 rounds out the Ruby framework with three additions: first-class mailers that plug into the existing action and view layers, built-in internationalization with translate and localize helpers per app and slice, and Minitest support. The release also ships faster defaults and developer-experience improvements across logging and assets, with mailers offering in-memory delivery for tests and pluggable custom delivery methods.

title: How we built SmithDB's inverted index for full-text search
blurb: LangChain's second post on SmithDB full-text search covers how the inverted index is constructed, compacted, and queried, with construction done inline during ingestion so new runs are searchable within seconds. To speed sorting, the team interns each unique term to an integer ID so comparison cost scales with distinct terms rather than occurrences, cutting construction time roughly 2.2x in their benchmark.

title: Turning Scattered Data Into Queryable Segments at Scale: How Razorpay Built Its Customer Data Platform
blurb: Razorpay built an in-house Customer Data Platform that resolves identities across devices and payment instruments, then serves audience segments across 500M+ user profiles in under 30ms. The post details how the consent-native design keeps PII isolated to source systems to satisfy India's DPDPA, replacing a workflow where a custom Spark job once took 2-3 days to return a single segment.

title: Introducing Package Proxy: Supply-Chain Safety Checks Without Client-Side Software
blurb: Thinkst released Package Proxy, a Cloudflare-deployed proxy that runs inline supply-chain safety checks for npm, pip, uv, and cargo without any client-side agent. It applies heuristics like flagging very recently published versions and packages with low install counts to catch back-doored or typo-squatted dependencies, and ships as an open-source repo you deploy into your own Cloudflare account.

title: video-use Review: browser-use Team's AI Video Editor
blurb: video-use is a 100% open-source skill that turns Claude Code, Codex, or any coding agent with shell access into a video editor, transcribing raw footage into a phrase-level Markdown transcript so the LLM reads the video instead of watching 45M tokens of frames, then expresses cuts as an edl.json that plain ffmpeg renders. Shipped in April 2026, it has passed 13,000 GitHub stars, adding over 3,000 in a single week.

title: Building My Own Self-Hosted dbt Cloud
blurb: A data engineer rebuilt the most-used parts of dbt Cloud as a self-hosted stack, wiring a React/Shadcn front end and FastAPI backend over dbt Core and a self-hosted Prefect API for orchestration. The build targets the three features the author sees used most, a browser IDE, job scheduling, and run history, and starts with the hardest piece: an in-browser editor with git logic and shell-command execution.

title: Kafka Share Groups - Pathological Fetch Waits with Record_limit
blurb: The author traces why Kafka share groups with share.acquire.mode=record_limit and fewer consumers than partitions consistently stalled out his Dimster throughput tests, isolating the cause to round-robin fetching that polls one broker at a time rather than concurrently. The post shows how this fetch pattern, combined with partition skew, produces pathological wait times that block lag drains, with batch_optimized avoiding the problem by fetching brokers in parallel.

title: Build from anywhere with Cursor for iOS
blurb: Cursor launched a native iOS app in public beta that lets developers start and steer coding agents from their phone, either always-on in the cloud or via Remote Control over agents running on their own machine. The app supports any frontier model, voice input, and slash commands, and pushes lock-screen Live Activities plus notifications when an agent finishes, needs input, or has a PR ready to merge.

title: SAGE: Structured Agentic Graph Editing for Software Diagrams
blurb: SAGE is a browser-based tool for editing draw.io and Mermaid software diagrams through natural language prompts while preserving layout, editable structure, and semantic relationships. It maps each diagram to an editable graph, translates requests into structured edit intents, compiles those into graph operations, and validates and repairs the resulting XML, with successful edits stored as versioned artifacts and evaluated against a Kubernetes architecture case study.

title: AutoRestTest at the SBFT 2026 Tool Competition
blurb: AutoRestTest is a black-box REST API fuzzer that pairs a Semantic Property Dependency Graph with multi-agent reinforcement learning and LLMs to explore large input spaces and inter-operation dependencies. It ranked first in fault detection, efficiency, and effectiveness at the SBFT 2026 REST League, averaging 67.09 unique server errors across 11 APIs and 317 operations under a one-hour budget.

title: You Shall Not Pass! Where and Why Developers Draw The Line on AI Autonomy
blurb: A study of 448 professional developers at Microsoft found that most accept AI producing work under their oversight, but acceptance drops sharply for identity-defining, human-facing, and design-oriented tasks. Developers were less willing to grant autonomy on work they feel accountable for or that defines their role, while higher task demands and more AI experience pushed acceptance up.

title: LLVM-Bench: Benchmarking and Advancing Large Language Models for LLVM Compiler Issue Resolution
blurb: This paper introduces LLVM-Bench, the first large-scale benchmark for LLVM compiler issue resolution, with 423 validated real-world tasks and LLVM-Gym, a platform that automates issue reproduction, patch application, compiler builds, and test runs. Across four LLMs, six retrieval setups, and three agents, current techniques stall out with patch invalidity and build failures dominating, though the LLVM-Ens ensemble that pools and filters diverse patches lifts the resolution rate to 21.99%.

title: Beyond Static Prompts: Building Scale-Proof, Polymorphic Multi-Agent Systems with Google's ADK
blurb: Google's ADK guide replaces static prompt-loading of all schemas with a metadata registry that injects schemas at runtime, cutting context bloat and attention diffusion.

title: Google Antigravity agents get full context with GitLab Orbit
blurb: GitLab released Orbit, a lifecycle context graph that installs from the Google Antigravity MCP Store and gives coding agents structured access to projects, pipelines, merge requests, vulnerabilities, and source code across a GitLab instance. Orbit indexes the instance into a knowledge graph exposed through two MCP tools, query_graph and get_graph_schema, and in internal tests cut agent hallucinations by up to 45x while using up to 4.5x fewer tokens.

title: Top 1 Million Analysis – June 2026: Ten Years of Web Security
blurb: Scott Helme's ten-year retrospective crawls the Tranco Top 1M, tracking HTTPS, HSTS, CSP, and security-header adoption since 2016.

title: Petrify: Petri-net Based Analysis of Concurrency Properties in Java Bytecode
blurb: Petrify encodes Java bytecode into Petri nets for model-checking concurrency properties like deadlock, working across any Java version and some Kotlin.

title: Towards Better Linux Kernel Fault Localization: Leveraging Contrastive Reasoning and Hierarchical Context Analysis
blurb: CoHiKer is an LLM-based fault localization technique built for the Linux kernel that pairs contrastive reasoning over mutated passing and failing test cases with hierarchical context analysis that narrows scope from files to methods using crash reports, syscall semantics, and inter-file dependencies. Against five baselines it lifts Top-1 method-level accuracy by up to 56.85% while cutting token consumption by up to 28.9%, and it generalizes to non-kernel code with smaller gains.

title: Godot says bye bye AI, bans vibe-coded contributions
blurb: The Godot game engine banned AI-generated contributions to its codebase, citing copyright ambiguity, low-quality "vibe-coded" pull requests, and the maintenance burden of reviewing submissions their authors don't fully understand. The policy reflects a growing split in open source over whether AI assistance helps or floods projects with plausible-looking code that shifts the review cost onto human maintainers.

title: Qualcomm Linux 2.0
blurb: Qualcomm released Linux 2.0, a unified IoT operating system that collapses the previous Base and Custom variants into a single kernel and rootfs across its Dragonwing SoCs, built on Linux 6.18 LTS and Yocto 6.0 Wrynose.
