Development now happens in the open on GitHub with public CI and modular meta-qcom overlays for audio, camera, and vision, plus out-of-the-box PREEMPT_RT support that replaces the old configure-it-yourself real-time layer.

title: Introducing Flink's Native S3 FileSystem: Built for Performance, Designed for Production
blurb: Apache Flink 2.3 introduced flink-s3-fs-native, a Hadoop-free S3 filesystem built specifically for Flink that ships as an experimental opt-in plugin and supports both RecoverableWriter for exactly-once sinks and a fast read path for checkpointing. The native plugin ends the long-standing choice between the Hadoop and Presto plugins, drops the hadoop-common dependency tree and AWS SDK v1, and delivers measurable performance gains already running in production at scale.

title: RACORN-1: Adaptive Recall-Preserving Speedup for Low-Selectivity Filtered Vector Search
blurb: RACORN-1 extends ACORN-1, the filtered-vector-search method that reuses an existing HNSW index, to fix the recall collapse that hits below 1% selectivity. Its Adaptive Search Fallback repurposes filter-failing nodes as temporary bridges around severed graph paths, recovering recall from as low as 0.03 back to 0.77-0.98 while keeping a 9-26x latency edge over HNSW. A RACORN-1+ variant adds exact fallback to hit perfect recall at extreme selectivity, making filtered search over RAG metadata predicates viable without rebuilding the index.

title: Query-Centric Optimization of AI Workflows via Approximate Query Processing and Proxy Models
blurb: This paper reframes expensive AI workflows, from LLM post-training to agentic reasoning, as declarative queries whose costly predicate is a large model, then applies two classic database tricks to cut invocations. Approximate query processing samples records and stops early once a confidence interval stabilizes, hitting 10-15% of oracle calls on TPC-DS with under 10% error; a CPU-resident decision tree pre-filters confident cases, cutting reward-model scoring time up to 19x on structured tasks with under 10% accuracy loss.

title: Granular Usage Attribution for dbt Pipelines with Query Tags
blurb: Databricks moved Query Tags to Public Preview, letting the dbt-databricks adapter (1.11+) auto-inject metadata like dbt_model_name into every query and attach custom tags for team, cost center, or environment. Tags land in system.query.history as a MAP<STRING, STRING>, turning per-model cost attribution and performance debugging into a plain SQL query. For teams staring at a doubled warehouse bill and 80 identical "Databricks Dbt" rows, it replaces guesswork with model-level spend visibility at zero configuration.

title: Historical memory prices 1960-2026
blurb: An interactive Stanford dataset charting DRAM, NAND, and HBM prices per gigabyte from 1960 to 2026, with downloadable raw data.

title: How we keep GPUs reliable across Databricks AI
blurb: Databricks published the first post in a series on keeping GPUs reliable at training scale, covering the failure modes its infrastructure catches and the multi-stage health checks that surface them. The engineering team sorts most GPU failures into three buckets: crashed jobs, silent slowdowns, and numerical corruption. Crashes announce themselves through NCCL watchdog timeouts, but the harder cases are runs that finish with corrupted weights or grind at degraded speed for hours unnoticed.

title: Generative Retrieval for Table Union Search
blurb: GenTUS reframes table union search as constrained generation, assigning each data-lake table a compact unionability-aware identifier and training a model to emit the identifiers of unionable tables directly from a query. This drops the standard encode-search-rerank pipeline, whose quality is capped by candidate-pool recall and whose latency and storage grow with the lake. Across seven public TUS benchmarks it posts the best average rank, 1.05 versus 2.57 for the strongest baseline, while cutting online latency, retrieval-artifact storage, and incremental update cost.

title: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search with Graph Range Filters
blurb: This paper tackles filtered ANN search where the constraint is graph proximity, requiring retrieved vectors to fall within a set distance of the query node in a predefined filter graph rather than a numeric range or category match. The proposed DLH index uses distance-aware labeling sets with Bloom filter compression to speed up the set intersections these queries need, and a memoized variant, DLH-M, caches the query node's hashing index. Across datasets, the two lift throughput by up to 70.3% while holding recall above 98.5%.

title: Exploring the Semantic Gap in Agentic Data Systems: A Formative Study of Operationalization Failures in Analytical Workflows
blurb: A cross-domain study of LLM-generated analytical workflows found that 153 of 236 analytical intents failed to operationalize correctly even when the workflow generated and executed cleanly, across finance, HR, and public safety domains. The authors group the failures into five classes, including comparative grounding, quantitative reasoning, and policy grounding, arguing that schemas and data values alone don't carry the semantic information needed to map user intent to correct computation. The implication is that agentic data systems will need richer semantic layers, not just better query generation.

title: TVA: A Version-aware Temporal Graph Storage System for Real-time Analytics
blurb: This paper introduces TVA, a temporal graph storage system that separates version metadata from property values so queries can locate valid versions and dereference the data directly. Tailored structures, a temporal table and hopscotch-based hashing, keep adjacent version metadata compact to cut random I/O during neighborhood scans, while a version-skipping strategy reuses temporal information across scans. The authors report up to 9.9x lower query latency and 2.2x lower storage overhead than state-of-the-art temporal graph stores.

title: When to Repair a Graph ANN Index: Navigability-Signal-Triggered Local Repair Protects Tail Recall Under Bursty Churn
blurb: This paper argues that graph ANN indexes like HNSW and DiskANN should trigger repair on a measured navigability-degradation signal rather than a fixed "consolidate every X operations" clock. At matched repair budget on SIFT-128 and Fashion-MNIST, signal-triggered repair Pareto-dominates fixed-cadence, lifting worst-case recall@10 by +0.014 to +0.050 while mean recall barely moves. The gain concentrates on sparse, fragile graphs at scarce budget and fades to parity when the index is robust.

title: When Classic Cache Policies Fail: Learning-Augmented Replacement for Semantic Retrieval Buffers
blurb:
This paper formalizes semantic retrieval buffers as an online cache replacement problem where hits are graded by embedding similarity rather than exact match. Testing eight policies on MemoryBench-Full, the authors find LRU and LFU consistently lose to plain FIFO, since semantic workloads lack temporal locality and frequency concentration. Their SOLAR framework uses regret-based eviction timing and Bayesian feedback to beat FIFO by 5-75% at tight cache sizes, with a proven competitive ratio of 3 or less.

(78 words)

title: Clean Me If You Can: A Large Collection of Real-World Addresses for Data Cleaning Benchmarking
blurb: This paper releases a large collection of real-world postal addresses paired with ground-truth clean values, targeting the shortage of dirty datasets for benchmarking data cleaning methods. The authors show that existing error-detection and correction approaches degrade sharply on the messy addresses, and they use those failures to lay out guidelines for future cleaning research.

title: The Decode-Work Law: Margin-Governed, Provably-Exact Spatial Joins over Compressed Geometry
blurb: This paper models a cost that filter-and-refine spatial joins have always ignored: the bytes spent decompressing candidate pairs that survive the filter. Running provably-exact polygon intersection joins over a Douglas-Peucker LOD ladder on TIGER water polygons, the certificate decodes a median 5.9x fewer vertices than decompress-then-refine, with zero correctness violations. The core finding, the "decode-work law," is that decode cost tracks each pair's distance from the predicate-flip boundary, independent of object size.

title: Free The Icons
blurb: Rogue Amoeba's Paul Kafasis argues Apple should end MacOS 26 Tahoe's mandate that every app icon conform to a single squircle, a rule that shrinks noncompliant icons into gray-boxed "icon jail" and flattens decades of distinctive Mac icon shapes. He notes the uniformity also hurts usability, since identical shapes leave color as the only way to distinguish apps at a glance, which fails for anyone with color vision deficiency.

title: Figma Grows 46% and Trades at 6x. Everyone Else Growing 30%+ Trades at 15-36x.
blurb: Figma trades near $19 with a market cap around $10B, down roughly 87% from its post-IPO peak even as revenue growth accelerated to 46% year over year with 82% gross margins and 139% net dollar retention. Backing out cash, the design-tool leader sits at about 6x forward revenue while software peers growing 30%-plus command 15x to 36x, a gap the market pins on AI fear over prompt-to-interface tools.

title: No, you haven't accidentally downloaded a banking app – that really is the new Winter Olympics 2030 logo
blurb: Alpes 2030 revealed the Winter Olympics identity, a mark of radiant lines converging into an alpine peak rendered in ice-blue and alpenglow pink gradients, with the Paralympic version inverting the same shape into a connected system. The execution is clean and scales from venue signage to avatars, though its geometric minimalism reads closer to a fintech brand than a winter sports event.

title: The <Usermedia> HTML Element
blurb: Chrome introduced the `<usermedia>` HTML element, shipping in Chrome 151 as the second entry in the Capability Elements suite after `<geolocation>`, replacing script-triggered getUserMedia() prompts with a declarative, user-activated control that delivers the MediaStream directly. Origin Trial data backs the shift: Zoom saw a 46.9% drop in camera and mic capture errors, and Google Meet reported a 131% increase in permission recovery for users who had previously denied access.

title: Draped Surfaces: A Contour-Adaptive Interface Overlaid on the Physical Environment for Mixed Reality Workspaces
blurb: This paper introduces CAMEO, a mixed-reality interface that drapes virtual windows onto physical surfaces instead of floating them in cockpit-style layouts, reducing occlusion of real-world objects. Two controlled studies found that draping cuts hand-movement detours when reaching for nearby items, while the surface deformation it requires does not measurably hurt text legibility.

title: Getty Images is canceling its $3.7 billion Shutterstock merger due to UK restrictions
blurb: Getty Images terminated its $3.7B merger with Shutterstock after the UK's Competition and Markets Authority demanded Shutterstock divest its global editorial business, including its celebrity and news photo agencies, as a condition of approval. The collapse leaves both stock-photo giants to face AI image competition alone, and shows that US antitrust clearance no longer guarantees a cross-border deal survives regulators elsewhere.

title: Touching and Feeling the Data: A Reusable Software Pipeline for Tactile Statistical Graphs in Accessible Education
blurb: This paper introduces a three-layer JavaScript pipeline that turns statistical charts into 3D-printable tactile graphs for blind and low-vision students, automating a process that previously took hours of manual CAD work per graph. It derives haptic design parameters from tactile-perception research, ships modular builders for scatter, bar, histogram, line, and box plots, and outputs print-ready STL files in under 250 milliseconds, with optional LLM chart extraction gated behind mandatory teacher review.

title: Open Studio designs izakaya-style restaurant with "half-dipped dumpling" lamp
blurb: Open Studio designed Dumpling Darlings, a small-plates izakaya in Singapore's New Bahru mall, around an oversized fibreglass dumpling lamp half-dipped through a cut-out in the low corrugated-steel roof. The lamp reinterprets the red akachōchin lantern as a beacon, while plywood booths with metal joints and custom Gamar chairs carry the same tectonic logic through a compact 100-square-metre interior.

title: Gustaf Westman creates colourful room settings for Selfridges pop-up store
blurb: Gustaf Westman built IKEA-style room settings for a month-long Selfridges Corner Shop pop-up, filling a living room, kitchen, and sticker-covered pink bedroom with his signature bulbous, colourful pieces alongside British-specific debuts like a tea strainer, a fish-and-chips holder, and his first patterned fabric in Tartan. The freestanding, center-of-room layout marks a shift from his previous pop-ups staged inside real apartments across Europe.

title: Fifth Avenue food hall identity highlights how a person can trump a mood board
blurb: Consultancy Love & War branded Shaver Hall, a food hall inside the old Lord & Taylor flagship on Fifth Avenue, by naming it after Dorothy Shaver, the store's forgotten pioneering president rather than an invented concept. The case study argues that a real, overlooked person gives a heritage project built-in credibility and press hooks, while the identity borrows the building's art deco grammar instead of competing with it.

title: Sunken lightwells illuminate partially submerged London home by Stylus Architects
blurb: Stylus Architects completed Green Lodge, a partially submerged family home in southwest London where south-facing sunken lightwells and a staircase rooflight bring daylight to bedrooms and bathrooms set below ground. Planning constraints on a heritage-heavy site near Putney Heath drove the design underground, leaving only a single-storey larch-clad volume with an asymmetric vaulted roof visible from the street.

title: OpenAI's first hardware isn't the revolutionary iPhone killer we expected
blurb: OpenAI's first hardware is the Codex Micro, a Work Louder macro pad for coding shortcuts, not the Jony Ive AI device the market expected.

title: This new logo design breaks so many rules, but I love it
blurb: SAND Agency's Grant Sanders redesigned the identity for the Association of Applied Paleontological Sciences, deliberately breaking logo orthodoxy with competing typefaces, a stencil "AAPS" wordmark, hand-applied blue script, and a detailed dinosaur illustration on aged cream paper. The result trades versatility and scalability for archival storytelling, leaning toward fashion-brand territory, with a separate simplified avatar handling the social and small-scale applications the main mark can't.

title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers analyzing badkeys data found a class of weak RSA keys with regularly spaced blocks of all zeros, including certificates issued to Yahoo, Verizon, and NetApp devices, plus SSH hosts running EnterpriseDT's CompleteFTP (RSA keys from v10.0.0-12.0.0, DSA keys from v10.0.0-23.0.4). The sparse moduli are factorable, and the recurrence of the same failure across independent implementations suggests more vulnerable products remain undiscovered and worth scanning for.

title: Over 900 Oracle E-Business instances exposed to ongoing attacks
blurb: Over 900 Oracle E-Business Suite instances are exposed online as threat actors actively exploit CVE-2026-46817 (CVSS 9.8), an unauthenticated HTTP takeover flaw in the File Transmission component of Oracle Payments. Oracle patched it in its May 2026 Critical Patch Update, but with in-the-wild exploitation now confirmed on honeypots and no public POC, edge-facing EBS operators should patch immediately and treat unpatched instances as compromised.

title: SharePoint RCE CVE-2026-45659 Added to CISA KEV After Active Exploitation
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-45659 (CVSS 8.8), a deserialization-of-untrusted-data RCE in Microsoft SharePoint Server, to its KEV catalog after confirming active exploitation. Any authenticated attacker with Site Member permissions can trigger it, so FCEB agencies must apply Microsoft's May 2026 patch by July 4 across Subscription Edition, 2019, and 2016 servers.

title: Cisco Confirms In-the-Wild Exploitation of Unified CM Vulnerability
blurb: Cisco confirmed active in-the-wild exploitation of CVE-2026-20230 (CVSS 8.6), an SSRF flaw in Unified CM and Unified CM SME that lets attackers write arbitrary files to the underlying OS and escalate to root. Only appliances with the non-default WebDialer service enabled are affected; patch to 14SU6 now, since public PoC code exists and 15SU5 won't ship until September.

title: Citrix Patches NetScaler Vulnerabilities, Including New ‘HTTP/2 Bomb’ Attack
blurb: Citrix patched six vulnerabilities in NetScaler ADC and Gateway, including CVE-2026-8451 (CVSS 8.8), a CitrixBleed-series XML parser out-of-bounds read that can leak restricted memory from appliances configured as SAML IDP. Exploitation requires specific preconditions and can be chained with a memory corruption bug for full device compromise, so self-managed deployments should apply the fixed versions (14.1-72.61, 13.1-63.18) immediately.

title: A new Android malware from Google
blurb: F-Droid published a polemic reframing Google's mandatory Android Developer Verification (ADV) program as device-wide malware, arguing the "Android Developer Verifier" system service that will gate app installs on Android 8+ functions as unremovable, centrally-controlled root-level control over what code users can run. The piece contends ADV does little to stop malware recidivism and instead entrenches Google as a chokepoint, pointing to federated-verifier alternatives as less draconian fixes.

title: CISA Warns of Actively Exploited Microsoft SharePoint Vulnerability
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-45659 (CVSS 8.8), a deserialization-of-untrusted-data RCE in Microsoft SharePoint Server, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation. The flaw lets an authenticated attacker with only Site Member permissions run arbitrary code, was patched out-of-band in late May, and now carries a three-day federal remediation deadline under BOD 26-04 for the Subscription Edition, 2019, and 2016 releases.

title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified Communications Manager (CVSS 8.6) that chains to unauthenticated root RCE, with attacks starting less than 24 hours after SSD Secure Disclosure published PoC and a full exploit chain. Cisco patched the bug on June 3; the WebDialer service is off by default, so prioritize patching and disabling WebDialer on any internet-exposed CUCM or Unified CM SME deployments.

title: HARC: Coupling Harmfulness and Refusal Directions for Robust Safety Alignment
blurb: This paper introduces HARC (Harmfulness-And-Refusal Coupling), a fine-tuning method that pairs an LLM's separable harmfulness and refusal directions across both prompt and response token positions to harden safety alignment. The authors show jailbreaks work by suppressing one direction before generation, and that models often recognize harmful content mid-response even after missing it at the prompt. HARC beat six training-time and inference-time baselines on the robustness-capability-usability trade-off, and the directions transferred across five model families and two scales without architecture-specific tuning.

title: U.S. lifting export control restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos, Fable
blurb: The U.S. Commerce Department lifted export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after the company agreed to deploy new guardrails and jailbreak classifiers, restoring foreign and domestic access. The controls followed an Amazon threat-intel report on jailbreaking Fable's cyber capabilities, though Anthropic's testing found ChatGPT 5.5, Claude Opus 4.8, and Kimi K2.7 could identify the same vulnerabilities and produce equivalent proof-of-concept code.

title: New ChocoPoC malware targets researchers via trojanized PoC exploits
blurb: A new campaign delivers ChocoPoC, a Python-based RAT, through at least seven trojanized PoC exploit repos on GitHub targeting security researchers with lures for fresh CVEs like FortiWeb (CVE-2025-64446) and PAN-OS (CVE-2026-0257). Rather than embedding malware in the exploit, the repos add a malicious PyPI dependency ('frint', pulling 'skytext', downloaded 2,400 times) that fetches ChocoPoC to steal browser credentials, shell history, and files.

title: The ARToken phishing panel targets Microsoft 365 accounts
blurb: Cisco Talos tied a phishing campaign spoofing real vendor accounts-payable contacts to EvilTokens, a subscription service ($1,500 up front plus a monthly fee) that abuses Microsoft's OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant to capture Microsoft 365 tokens and bypass MFA. Lures route victims to look-alike SharePoint tenants on genuine sharepoint.com hosts, and Sekoia has catalogued roughly 500 Cloudflare Workers domains tied to the platform targeting finance, HR, and logistics staff.

title: Anthropic restores Claude Fable 5 as US lifts export controls — single filter now blocks prompt that could identify software vulnerabilities and write code to exploit them
blurb: Anthropic restored global access to Claude Fable 5 on July 1, a day after the U.S. Department of Commerce withdrew the June 12 export controls that had barred foreign nationals, including Anthropic's own non-citizen staff, from using it. The fix was a single classifier that blocks the Amazon-reported prompt-to-exploit technique in over 99% of cases by rerouting flagged requests to Opus 4.8, after CAISI review confirmed weaker models could do the same thing.

title: AI Agent Exploits Langflow RCE to Automate Database Ransomware Attack
blurb: Sysdig's Threat Research Team attributes what it calls the first fully AI-agent-driven ransomware attack to JADEPUFFER, which exploited CVE-2025-3248, a missing-authentication RCE in Langflow, to gain a foothold. The LLM then autonomously harvested credentials, pivoted via CVE-2021-29441 and a default Nacos signing key to a root MySQL login, and encrypted 1,342 Nacos configs before dropping a Bitcoin ransom note.

title: Apple Patches Dozens of Vulnerabilities Across iOS, macOS, and Safari
blurb: Apple shipped iOS 26.5.2, iPadOS 26.5.2, and macOS Tahoe 26.5.2 with 37 security fixes, including 26 WebKit bugs that let a malicious website exfiltrate data, corrupt memory, hijack the clipboard, or escape the sandbox, alongside a Safari 26.5.2 update patching 31 flaws for Sonoma and Sequoia. Apple reports no in-the-wild exploitation, but WebKit bugs are routinely weaponized shortly after disclosure, so patch edge and endpoint devices now.

title: Opening up 'Zero-Knowledge Proof' technology to promote privacy in age assurance
blurb: Google open sourced its Zero-Knowledge Proof libraries (github.com/google/longfellow-zk), letting developers build age-assurance and digital-ID systems that prove a claim like "over 18" without disclosing any other data. The release targets EU eIDAS and EUDI Wallet integration, giving Member States and relying parties a performant ZKP implementation to embed privacy-preserving verification rather than harvesting identity attributes.

title: New ChocoPoC RAT Targets Vulnerability Researchers via Fake PoC Exploit Repos
blurb: A supply-chain campaign called ChocoPoC hides a Python remote-access trojan inside fake GitHub PoC repos claiming to exploit trending CVEs, targeting vulnerability researchers who race to test new flaws. The malware ships in a transitive dependency (frint pulling in skytext) that only detonates alongside a real PoC file, stealing browser credentials, cookies, and files before opening a shell; YesWeHack and Sekoia report the infrastructure is still live, so treat community PoCs as untrusted.

title: FortiBleed Credential Theft Linked to INC and Lynx Ransomware Operations
blurb: SOCRadar linked the FortiBleed credential-harvesting campaign to INC Ransom and Lynx ransomware, after finding an operator logged into both groups' negotiation panels from FortiBleed infrastructure. The operation scanned roughly 430,000 FortiGate firewalls, harvested over 110 million credentials via Golang packet sniffers on about 12,000 devices, and has produced at least 12 ransomware deployments so far.

title: Medtronic notifies customers impacted by ShinyHunters data breach
blurb: Medtronic is notifying customers that the ShinyHunters extortion group breached its corporate IT systems between April 13 and 19, exposing PII including names, dates of birth, Social Security numbers, and health-related data across roughly 9 million records. ShinyHunters listed the medical device maker on its dark web portal on April 18 but pulled the entry after threatening to publish; Medtronic says the stolen data was not posted online and is offering 24 months of credit monitoring.

title: Hackers shoveled snow for company, were rewarded with network admin access
blurb: Physical and social-engineering pretexting let attackers earn insider trust by posing as helpful contractors, in this case shoveling snow for a target company before parlaying that goodwill into network admin access. The tactic sidesteps technical controls entirely, exploiting the human tendency to reciprocate favors and grant access to familiar faces. Defenders should treat physical access, badge issuance, and account provisioning as identity-verification checkpoints, not courtesies extended to anyone who looks the part.

title: Beyond the Prompt: Jailbreaking Function-Calling LLMs via Simulated Moderation Traces
blurb: This paper introduces SMT (Simulated Moderation Traces), a black-box jailbreak that exploits function-calling LLMs by fabricating a multi-turn moderation-auditing workflow, treating safety refusals as execution failures to gradually erode the model's constraints. Tested against commercial models from five providers, SMT achieved the highest attack success rate with near-minimal queries, showing that prompt-level sanitization alone cannot defend tool-enabled systems without context-aware validation across schemas, arguments, and conversation state.

title: (A)I Sees What You Don't: Exploiting New Attack Surfaces in Third-Party Mobile Agents
blurb: Researchers disclosed seven attacks against third-party mobile agents built on Vision-Language Models, exploiting two novel surfaces: a Screen Perception surface that abuses the gap between human and machine vision (subliminal text injection, invisible pixel zones) and a Misused Channel surface that tampers with screenshots or injects host PC commands. Across five popular agent frameworks, a permissionless malicious app hijacked agent actions and achieved arbitrary command execution while remaining visually indistinguishable to the user.

title: KidnapRAG: A Black-Box Attack for Hijacking Reasoning in Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
blurb: KidnapRAG is a black-box poisoning attack that hijacks the multi-step reasoning chain in Agentic RAG systems using three role-specific documents (Bait, Chain-Link, Mal-Ins) that attract retrieval, force query reformulation, and inject attacker-controlled evidence. The attacker needs only the ability to publish externally retrievable documents, with no access to system prompts, retrievers, or model parameters, and it outperforms existing poisoning baselines across multiple frameworks and LLM backbones.

title: Opera rolls out Paste Protect feature to fight ClickFix attacks
blurb: Opera released Paste Protect, a browser feature that scans clipboard content against platform-specific rules on Windows, macOS, and Linux to block ClickFix-style attacks that trick users into pasting attacker-supplied commands into a terminal. Blocked copies trigger a warning, a red address-bar indicator, and a 5-second override, with allow-lists for trusted sources like GitHub.

title: Microsoft Accelerates Post-Quantum Cryptography Shift to 2029
blurb: Microsoft accelerated its Quantum Safe Program timeline, targeting migration of critical products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029, with priorities including TLS 1.3 adoption, crypto-agility for stored data, and PQC-secured trust chains like code signing and certificate issuance. The move follows a Trump executive order setting hard PQC deadlines for federal agencies, signaling that quantum-safe migration is becoming a near-term compliance requirement rather than a long-horizon planning exercise.

title: All-out Attack: Optimal Block Withholding Under Pay-Per-Share Scheme
blurb: This paper analyzes Block Withholding under Pay-Per-Share (PPS) and Full-PPS mining pool schemes, proving they are not incentive compatible. The optimal strategy, dubbed the All-out Attack, commits all adversarial hashpower to a victim pool, submitting only partial shares while withholding valid blocks. After the first difficulty adjustment, the attacker gains α/(1-α) relative to pre-adjustment rates while the pool operator pays out-of-pocket for shares and receives no blocks in return. Fork After Withholding variants yield no additional profit.

title: This phishing kit looks more like BEC-as-a-service
blurb: Cisco Talos disclosed ARToken, a BEC-as-a-service operator panel that runs as an affiliate of the EvilTokens MFA-bypass phishing operation and adds inbox rule manipulation, shared access links, and a seven-layer anti-analysis system to compromise Microsoft 365 accounts. Its lures are targeted rather than scattershot, spoofing accounts-payable contacts at real vendors with outstanding-invoice requests, so defenders should tighten mailbox rule auditing and enforce phishing-resistant MFA.

title: Catching ransomware on the wire before it locks the file server
blurb: Researchers at La Trobe University built a ransomware detection framework that reads SMB file-sharing traffic on the wire rather than relying on endpoint agents, closing the blind spot where a compromised client encrypts files on a remote server. The method segments traffic using fixed-size SMB control packets, such as the 260-byte directory enumeration and 410-byte new-file response, to reconstruct client file operations and fingerprint ransomware families by note size, without inspecting packet contents or touching endpoints.

title: Antaeus: Hunting Repository-Level Logic Vulnerabilities via Context-Grounded LLM Reasoning
blurb: Antaeus is a framework for detecting repository-level logic vulnerabilities that grounds LLM reasoning in whole-repo context, prioritizing functions with lightweight security signals before running costly analysis on security-sensitive sinks and their safety conditions. Across 28 repositories with confirmed logic bugs, it detected and explained 15 vulnerabilities, outperforming function-level and agentic baselines at comparable token usage and cost.

title: EvilTokens device-code phishing kit totally more evil than we all thought
blurb: Researchers detailed EvilTokens, a phishing kit that automates OAuth device-code flow abuse to harvest access and refresh tokens, letting attackers bypass MFA and maintain persistent access without ever capturing a password. Defenders should disable device-code authorization where it isn't needed, enforce conditional-access policies that restrict token issuance to managed devices, and hunt for anomalous device-code grant events in sign-in logs.

title: Distill to Detect: Exposing Stealth Biases in LLMs through Cartridge Distillation
blurb: New method distills the logit-distribution gap between a suspect LLM and its base to surface stealth preferential biases invisible to text inspection.

title: Where Severity Scores Go Wrong: “Just Add Prototype Pollution”
blurb: JFrog's Security Research team found that CVSS severity scores routinely overstate real-world risk, reassessing 2025 NVD data and downgrading 96% of critical and 65% of high-severity CVEs after weighing practical exploitation requirements. Prototype pollution is the case study: the 10 CVEs Axios has disclosed since April 2026 all require a separate, pre-existing prototype pollution flaw in the process to exploit, yet carry inflated scores that ignore that dependency. Prioritize patching against actual exploitability and environmental constraints, not headline CVSS numbers.

title: ReShift: Aha-Moment-Driven Reasoning-Level Backdoor Attacks on Vision-Language Models
blurb: This paper introduces ReShift, a reasoning-level backdoor for vision-language models that redirects the internal chain-of-thought while keeping the visible reasoning trace coherent, evading defenses that flag inconsistent or malformed traces. The authors report high attack success rates with preserved clean-task performance, using a poisoned-reasoning data pipeline and joint supervised-reinforcement optimization to make trigger-conditioned reasoning shifts stable and hard to detect.

title: Google testing controversial webcam-based reCAPTCHA that asks for a hand scan to prove you're human — testers beat it with a stock photo
blurb: Google is testing a webcam-based reCAPTCHA that activates the user's camera and asks for an open-palm gesture, mapping 21 hand-landmark points to decide whether a real person is present. Testers defeated it within days by feeding a static stock photo through OBS Virtual Camera, clearing the challenge with no live person or video and adding user friction without meaningful bot resistance.

title: The Rise and Fall of Google's Privacy Sandbox
blurb: Google retired most Privacy Sandbox APIs on October 17, 2025, ending nearly five years of work on a privacy-preserving alternative to cross-site tracking. This paper's consent-aware crawls of the top 10,000 sites show adoption had already stagnated before the announcement, with only CHIPS still in use and the challenge of privacy-preserving interest-based advertising left unresolved.

title: Safe Alone, Unsafe Together: Safeguarding Against Implicit Toxicity When Benign Images Combine
blurb: This paper defines multi-image implicit toxicity (MIIT), where individual images pass moderation but harmful semantics emerge only when the images are interpreted together, a gap that current commercial moderation APIs miss. The authors release an image-only MIIT dataset across seven risk categories and train MiShield, whose 8B model outperforms larger models and commercial moderation services while producing explicit analyses of the correlated entities behind each flagged hazard.

title: Knowledge-Enhanced Agentic Vulnerability Repair
blurb: KeaRepair is an agentic automated vulnerability repair system that grounds patch generation in verified program facts and knowledge mined from historical vulnerability-patch pairs. A tool-augmented agent runs ReAct-style diagnosis, then generates patches validated through a closed loop of compilation, PoC replay, and test-suite execution. Paired with Gemini-3.1-Pro, it repaired 46 of 55 reproducible C/C++ vulnerabilities (83.64%), including six that no baseline could fix, signaling that remediation is closing the gap on AI-accelerated discovery.

title: GitHub’s new tool helps prevent costly open-source license violations
blurb: GitHub released License Compliance, now in public preview for GitHub Advanced Security customers, which scans dependencies added in pull requests and flags those whose licenses violate organizational policy. Teams define an allowlist of acceptable licenses (MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD-3-Clause), then run it in Evaluate mode to annotate PRs without blocking merges before enforcing exceptions per package.

title: Nissan employee data exposed in Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day attacks
blurb: Nissan disclosed a data breach after threat actors, linked to ShinyHunters, exploited a zero-day in Oracle PeopleSoft to exfiltrate current and former employee records across the US, Canada, Mexico, and Brazil. Exposed data includes Social Security numbers, banking details, and tax information; affected staff should rotate banking credentials and watch for phishing, as over 100 organizations have been hit in the same campaign.

title: AI researchers trick chatbots into sharing how to make cocaine as long as they believe a user is wearing a green shirt — 'CoT Forgery' exploit spurs LLMs to divulge forbidden info by faking trusted chains of thought
blurb: A new paper, "Prompt Injection as Role Confusion," describes CoT Forgery, an attack that wraps requests in fabricated chain-of-thought so models treat injected text as their own trusted reasoning, lifting jailbreak success from near zero to roughly 60% across every model tested. The technique works because LLMs infer a segment's authority from writing style rather than role tags, and stripping the stylistic markers while preserving meaning defeats it.

title: Robust Text Watermarking for Large Language Models via Dual Semantic Embeddings
blurb: DEW watermarks LLM text via dual semantic embeddings, staying detectable after paraphrasing and translation where prior schemes degrade.

title: What the AI patch gap means for enterprise security
blurb: Tuskira analyzed findings from Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview, which verified 1,596 vulnerabilities across hundreds of open-source projects in nine weeks at a 90.8% true-positive rate. Discovery ran at roughly 25 verified bugs a day while credited fixes moved at about 1.5, a 16.5-to-1 deficit that widens the open-issue backlog daily. Some 95% of disclosures had no public advisory at snapshot, so defenders should track upstream commits directly and operate at discovery cadence rather than waiting on advisory feeds.

title: No Country for Old Privacy: The Evolving Challenges of Anonymity in Bitcoin
blurb: A longitudinal study of Bitcoin's second-generation anonymization protocols (CoinJoin, CoinSwap, CoinShuffle, Stealth Addresses) found detectable privacy usage stays below 1% of network transactions, with sharp declines following key regulatory events. The authors identified 5.94M CoinJoin and 23.3M CoinSwap transactions via heuristic filters but found no standardized Stealth Address adoption, suggesting privacy-seeking users have migrated to less detectable methods rather than abandoning anonymity. For chain-analysis and threat-intel teams, on-chain visibility of these protocols understates real obfuscation activity.

title: Apple's Hide My Email service reportedly reveals users' actual email addresses with little effort — Cupertino has seemingly known about the problem for a year but has yet to fix it
blurb: Apple's Hide My Email aliases can be resolved to users' real addresses with a 100% success rate and minimal effort, per testing by 404 Media and researcher Tyler Murphy, who disclosed the flaw in June 2025. No CVE or exploit mechanism has been published, and Apple's March 2026 fix failed to resolve it, leaving an estimated 10M-plus paid subscribers relying on the feature exposed.

title: US Government allows Anthropic limited release of AI model that sparked cybersecurity concerns
blurb: The US government granted Anthropic a limited license to redeploy Claude Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, to a small group of vetted cyber defenders and infrastructure providers, reversing the export block ordered earlier in June. The reprieve does not cover the less powerful Fable 5, which remains suspended for general use as negotiations continue over the safeguards governing both models.

title: Papa Johns Surveillance-Based Advertising
blurb: Papa Johns partnered with NBCUniversal, Instacart, and dentsu agency Carat to target streaming ads at consumers predicted to be low on groceries, using Instacart purchase data on staples like eggs, milk, and meat to model when shoppers run out. The campaign served food-preference-specific creatives with prompts like "Empty fridge?", extending retail-data surveillance into behavioral ad timing.

title: Detecting Adversarial Evasion Attacks Against Autoencoder-Based Network Intrusion Detection Systems
blurb: This paper proposes two complementary detectors for adversarial evasion attacks against autoencoder-based network intrusion detection systems, targeting PANDA-style attacks that convert packet sequences into invertible grayscale images to run masked FGSM against NIDS anomaly scoring. The Residual Localisation Detector tracks spatial concentration of reconstruction errors in the inter-arrival time region, while the Feature-Space Perturbation Consistency Detector checks packet-level timing features directly. Both hit F1 ≥ 0.99 against adversarial traffic across IoT devices in the UQ-IoT dataset.

title: Kubota says hackers had month-long access to network systems
blurb: Kubota disclosed that a threat actor had access to some of its network systems for over a month, from March 16 to April 20, exfiltrating files containing personal data for employees and their dependents. Exposed data includes Social Security numbers, dates of birth, taxpayer IDs, driver's license numbers, and direct deposit bank account details; no ransomware group has claimed the attack, and Kubota began notifying affected individuals on June 30.

title: Apple Fixes WebKit Flaws in iOS and macOS, With Help From AI Tools
blurb: Apple shipped iOS 26.5.2, iPadOS 26.5.2, macOS Tahoe 26.5.2, and Safari 26.5.2, patching roughly 30 WebKit bugs plus three kernel flaws, four of which were found using AI tools including Anthropic's Claude and OpenAI's Codex Security. Apple broke from its usual practice of bundling fixes into the next major release, telling Reuters it compressed the timeline because AI can now turn a disclosed flaw into a working exploit faster.

title: SoK: Attack and Defense Landscape of Mobile On-device AI Systems
blurb: This paper presents the first systematization of knowledge (SoK) on security for mobile on-device AI (MoAI) systems, which run inference locally instead of in the cloud and thereby expose models to attacks stemming from local storage. The authors map the attack and defense landscape across MoAI security pillars, then flag unresolved gaps for teams shipping on-device inference to reason about model extraction, tampering, and other risks that don't exist in cloud-hosted deployments.

title: Opera’s new Paste Protect feature stops the clipboard attack your antivirus can’t catch
blurb: Opera released Paste Protect, a browser-native defense against ClickFix and clipboard-hijacking attacks that screens clipboard content in real time and blocks the copy when it matches known malicious-script patterns on Windows, macOS, and Linux. ClickFix, which tricks users into pasting attacker-supplied commands into a terminal, drove over 53% of malware loader incidents last year per Huntress, and no major browser previously shipped a native defense.

title: A Non-Line-of-Sight, Multi-Modality-based Side-Channel IP Theft Attack on Additive Manufacturing Using Dual Smartphones
blurb: Researchers demonstrated a non-line-of-sight side-channel attack that reconstructs a 3D printer's G-code at 98.89% command-level accuracy using two smartphones placed 60 cm away to capture acoustic and magnetic emissions. The technique steals additive-manufacturing IP without specialized hardware and transfers across printers and environments, so operators should treat printer acoustic and electromagnetic emanations as a leakage channel and physically isolate or shield production machines.

title: Minos: A Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework for Provenance-Based Backward Tracking
blurb: This paper introduces Minos, a multi-agent framework that reframes provenance-based backward tracking for APT forensics as an LLM-driven reasoning process, coordinating four specialized agents under a finite state machine to prune the search space instead of exhaustive graph traversal. Across 14 attack scenarios in five public datasets, it reached 0.92 recall and 0.64 precision while producing attack subgraphs 49% more compact than prior methods, and it emits interpretable reasoning traces to support forensic auditing.

title: Amazon fined $2.25M for withholding evidence from fraud victims
blurb: The FTC fined Amazon $2.25M to settle charges that it violated the Fair Credit Reporting Act by withholding fraudulent-transaction records from identity theft victims and law enforcement, sometimes citing "privacy" or "security" as the reason for denial. The proposed order requires Amazon to provide lawfully requested records to victims and authorized law enforcement within the FCRA's 30-day window and to notify consumers who were denied records since April 2024.

title: Securing the code that decides who Entra trusts: a Function App and a Logic App, end to end
blurb: Custom claims providers and other Entra extension points route token issuance through Azure resources you own, such as a Function App or Logic App, making that code an effective Tier 0 asset. A compromised Function App becomes a claim forger inside the token pipeline, since downstream apps trust its returned claims as if Entra asserted them. Place these hosts on the Control Plane, lock down their source repos and RBAC inheritance, and scope the outbound credential they use to call upstream sources.

title: Know Thy Neighbor: Cross-TEE Mutual Attestation
blurb: This paper introduces Hema, a formally-verified protocol for mutual attestation between trusted-application instances running on the same or different TEE types. The current default requires each TEE to embed the full attestation software stack of every other TEE it talks to (SGX carrying TrustZone's stack and vice versa), which Hema replaces with a single cross-TEE handshake. For teams running confidential-computing workloads across heterogeneous enclaves, this cuts the attestation surface each TEE must trust and verify.

title: Federated Sovereign Transport Protocol (FSTP): Verifiable Coordination Without Disclosure
blurb: This paper introduces the Federated Sovereign Transport Protocol (FSTP), a federation transport layer that makes data confinement a structural property rather than an operator policy. A synchronization agent with a formally closed output type set prevents raw internal data from appearing in any federation message, enforced by the Rust type system at compile time instead of runtime checks. A contextual identity model derives unlinkable per-relationship identifiers to block cross-context correlation, and a Blocklace event substrate provides tamper-evident partial ordering that supports erasure without breaking the hash chain.

title: SessionBound: Turning Enterprise Task Approval into Budgeted Database Sessions
blurb: SessionBound converts approved enterprise tasks into short-lived, budgeted database sessions for AI agents, so an agent can generate SQL freely but every query must execute inside a signed task token's boundary. Its SessionBoundDB runtime enforces safe views, row scope, denied fields, and query and disclosure budgets at the database layer rather than trusting an LLM to judge safety. A PostgreSQL prototype cleared a 24-scenario suite with p50 overhead of roughly 1.4ms.

title: The US DOJ says Peter Stokes, a 19-year-old dual US-Estonian citizen, was extradited from Finland to face charges of participating in Scattered Spider hacks (Joe Warminsky/The Record)
blurb: The DOJ extradited Peter Stokes, a 19-year-old dual US-Estonian citizen, from Finland to Chicago to face charges tied to Scattered Spider intrusions. The arrest marks continued law-enforcement pressure on the loosely organized group behind SIM-swapping and social-engineering attacks against major US telecom, retail, and casino targets.

title: Forensic-Oriented Intrusion Detection Using Synthetic Network Traffic Data and Explainable Artificial Intelligence
blurb: This paper trains intrusion detection on synthetic network traffic, achieving F1-macro 0.96 on CICIDS2017 while keeping evidence court-defensible.

title: The Binary Tree Mechanism is Optimal for Approximate Differentially Private Continual Counting
blurb: A new proof shows the binary tree mechanism is asymptotically optimal for approximate differentially private continual counting.

title: Over 900 Oracle E-Business instances exposed to ongoing attacks
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-46817, a CVSS 9.8 unauthenticated takeover flaw in Oracle E-Business Suite's Payments component, with roughly 950 instances still exposed online and no public patch-adoption data. Oracle shipped a fix in its May 2026 Critical Patch Update, but the ongoing campaign follows Clop and ShinyHunters intrusions into Oracle EBS and PeopleSoft, making immediate patching an urgent priority for enterprise IT teams.

title: Citrix Patches NetScaler Vulnerabilities, Including New ‘HTTP/2 Bomb’ Attack
blurb: Citrix released NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway updates patching six vulnerabilities, including four high-severity out-of-bounds read and memory bugs and the new HTTP/2 Bomb DoS flaw. IT teams running self-managed NetScaler, Gateway, or Secure Private Access Hybrid deployments should patch now, prioritizing CVE-2026-8451, which watchTowr flags as the latest CitrixBleed defect and a path to full device compromise.

title: Cisco Confirms In-the-Wild Exploitation of Unified CM Vulnerability
blurb: Cisco confirmed active in-the-wild exploitation of CVE-2026-20230, an 8.6-severity SSRF flaw in Unified Communications Manager and Unified CM SME that can drop arbitrary files and escalate to root on affected appliances. Only systems with the non-default WebDialer service enabled are exposed, and Cisco urges upgrading to the patched 14SU6 release now, with fixes landing in 15SU5 in September.

title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
blurb: Attackers began exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified Communications Manager that grants unauthenticated remote attackers root access, less than 24 hours after SSD Secure Disclosure published a full exploit chain. CUCM serves roughly 30 million users globally, and Cisco shipped fixed versions on June 3, urging admins to patch deployments running the optional WebDialer service rather than wait on the 8.6 CVSS score.

title: SharePoint RCE CVE-2026-45659 Added to CISA KEV After Active Exploitation
blurb: CISA added SharePoint Server flaw CVE-2026-45659 (CVSS 8.8), a deserialization remote-code-execution bug, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation, and set a July 4 patch deadline for federal agencies. Microsoft fixed the flaw in May 2026 for Subscription Edition, Server 2019, and Enterprise Server 2016, but any authenticated user with Site Member permissions can trigger it, so on-prem SharePoint operators should prioritize the update.

title: CISA Warns of Actively Exploited Microsoft SharePoint Vulnerability
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-45659, a high-severity SharePoint Server deserialization bug patched out-of-band in late May, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, giving federal agencies three days to patch under BOD 26-04. The flaw lets an authenticated attacker with only Site Member permissions run arbitrary code, and it affects Subscription Edition, 2019, and 2016 servers that anchor enterprise document sharing and intranets.

title: The ARToken phishing panel targets Microsoft 365 accounts
blurb: Cisco Talos tied a wave of invoice-themed phishing emails targeting Microsoft 365 accounts-payable staff to EvilTokens, a $1,500-plus-monthly subscription service that abuses Microsoft's OAuth device code flow to steal tokens and bypass MFA. The lures spoof real vendor domains and route victims to look-alike SharePoint tenants on genuine sharepoint.com hosts, and Sekoia has already catalogued roughly 500 Cloudflare Workers domains tied to the platform.

title: Apple Patches Dozens of Vulnerabilities Across iOS, macOS, and Safari
blurb: Apple released iOS 26.5.2, iPadOS 26.5.2, macOS Tahoe 26.5.2, and Safari 26.5.2 with 37 security fixes, including 26 WebKit flaws that malicious websites could exploit to exfiltrate data, corrupt memory, or escape the sandbox. Apple credited AI tooling from Anthropic and OpenAI Codey Security researchers with surfacing at least four of the defects, and IT teams should prioritize the WebKit patches given Apple bugs are routinely weaponized shortly after disclosure.

title: Amazon EKS now supports control plane egress through your VPC
blurb: AWS added customer-routed control plane egress to Amazon EKS, letting teams route Kubernetes API Server outbound traffic—admission webhook callbacks, OIDC discovery lookups, and aggregate API server requests—through an ENI in their own VPC under existing security group, endpoint policy, and Network Firewall controls. The feature targets regulated buyers in government, financial services, and healthcare, and supports org-wide enforcement via AWS Organizations Service Control Policies using the eks:controlPlaneEgressMode condition key.

title: New ChocoPoC malware targets researchers via trojanized PoC exploits
blurb: Researchers at Sekoia and YesWeHack uncovered ChocoPoC, a Python-based RAT distributed through at least seven trojanized proof-of-concept exploits on GitHub that hide their payload in PyPI dependencies rather than the exploit code itself, exfiltrating browser credentials and data via abused Mapbox datasets. The malicious 'skytext' package was downloaded 2,400 times, mostly on Linux, with installs surging after each new vulnerability disclosure lured security researchers into testing the weaponized PoCs.

title: A new Android malware from Google
blurb: F-Droid published a polemic recasting Google's Android Developer Verification program as malware, arguing the mandatory developer-registration requirement blocks apps from anyone not centrally approved by Google rather than actually stopping malware distribution. The group contends the rule does little against its stated threat of repeat offenders and proposes lighter alternatives like enhanced Play Protect scanning or federated verifiers, framing the mandate as control dressed as security.

title: FortiBleed Credential Theft Linked to INC and Lynx Ransomware Operations
blurb: SOCRadar linked the FortiBleed credential-theft campaign to INC and Lynx ransomware operations, marking the first time mass FortiGate credential harvesting has been tied directly to ransomware deployment. The campaign scanned roughly 430,000 Fortinet firewalls and collected over 110 million credentials, producing admin access on 409 targets and at least 12 ransomware deployments that encrypted hundreds of endpoints across affected organizations.

title: Meta shares jump 9% on reported plan to offer AI infrastructure services
blurb: Meta reportedly plans to rent its excess AI data-center capacity to outside companies, potentially offering GPUs, hosted models, or access to its Muse Spark LLM as it spends up to $145B on capex this fiscal year. The move would put Meta in direct competition with CoreWeave and Nebius, whose shares fell 13.9% and 17% on the news while Meta's rose 8.8%.

title: Introducing Claude Sonnet 5
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, its most agentic Sonnet model, with performance close to Opus 4.8 at introductory API pricing of $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens through August 31, 2026. Sonnet 5 is now the default on Free and Pro plans and available to Max, Team, and Enterprise users, giving IT buyers Opus-tier agentic capability at a lower cost tier.

title: Medtronic notifies customers impacted by ShinyHunters data breach
blurb: Medtronic began notifying customers that the ShinyHunters extortion group breached its corporate IT systems in April 2026, exposing names, Social Security numbers, dates of birth, and health information for what the attackers claimed was 9 million records. The medical device maker, which reports $33.5B in annual revenue and operates in 150 countries, says the data was not published online and is offering affected customers 24 months of credit monitoring.

title: Hackers shoveled snow for company, were rewarded with network admin access
blurb: Attackers gained network administrator access to a target company after physically ingratiating themselves with staff by shoveling snow outside the building, according to The Register. The incident underscores that enterprise security programs weighted toward endpoint and network controls remain exposed to physical social engineering, where badge policies, visitor escorting, and staff awareness training are the actual controls that matter.

title: Catching ransomware on the wire before it locks the file server
blurb: Researchers at La Trobe University built a ransomware detection method that reads Server Message Block traffic on the wire, flagging attacks by the fixed packet sizes SMB produces for actions like file creation, reads, and renames, without touching endpoints or opening packet contents. The approach targets the blind spot endpoint agents leave when a compromised client encrypts files on a remote server, where neither the server nor the client agent sees the full attack.

title: Microsoft Accelerates Post-Quantum Cryptography Shift to 2029
blurb: Microsoft accelerated its Quantum Safe Program timeline, committing to move critical products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029 and folding PQC requirements into its Secure Future Initiative. Priorities include adopting TLS 1.3, building crypto-agility for stored data, and migrating trust chains like code signing and certificate issuance, giving enterprise customers a vendor-backed deadline days after Trump's executive order set federal PQC mandates.

title: EvilTokens device-code phishing kit totally more evil than we all thought
blurb: Security researchers detailed EvilTokens, a phishing kit that automates OAuth device-code attacks to capture authentication tokens and bypass multi-factor authentication on Microsoft 365 and Entra ID tenants. Enterprise IT teams relying on MFA alone remain exposed, since the stolen tokens grant persistent access without a password, making conditional-access policies that restrict device-code flows the practical mitigation.

title: T-Mobile moving tens of thousands of virtual machines off VMware amid lawsuit
blurb: T-Mobile is migrating tens of thousands of virtual machines running across roughly 303,140 CPU cores off VMware, while suing Broadcom in New York to force continued support for the perpetual licenses it bought in 2023. The carrier is fighting Broadcom's post-acquisition shift from perpetual licenses to pricier subscription bundles, a dispute that hands every large VMware shop a template for pushing back on forced re-licensing.

title: Apple plans five new iPhones through 2027, eyes Chinese-made chips amid foldable push, reports say
blurb: Apple plans at least five new iPhone models through the first half of 2027 and raised its foldable production target to 10 million units, while its scale in memory sourcing lets it secure components as an AI-driven shortage squeezes rivals like Xiaomi and Oppo below 100 million units. Apple is separately in talks to buy memory chips for China-market devices from CXMT and YMTC, both on a Pentagon list of firms tied to Beijing's military.

title: Opera rolls out Paste Protect feature to fight ClickFix attacks
blurb: Opera added Paste Protect to its browser, a feature that scans clipboard content for malicious command patterns and blocks ClickFix social-engineering attacks that trick users into pasting and running info-stealer scripts. The tool builds on Opera's 2021 Hijack protection, supports Windows, macOS, and Linux, and lets users allow-list trusted sites like GitHub to reduce friction for developers.

title: Anthropic touts new Claude Sonnet 5 model range, offering performance ‘close to that of Opus 4.8, but at lower prices’ – here’s what users can expect
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, its most agentic Sonnet model, which scores 63.2% on SWE-bench Pro versus 58.1% for Sonnet 4.6 and 69.2% for flagship Opus 4.8. Adjustable effort levels let enterprise buyers tune the cost-performance tradeoff, matching Opus 4.8 on some tasks at higher effort while cutting token spend at medium effort, which pressures teams to reassess where Opus-tier pricing is still justified.

title: Kubota says hackers had month-long access to network systems
blurb: Kubota North America disclosed that hackers accessed its network systems for more than a month, between March 16 and April 20, exposing employee and dependent data including Social Security numbers, taxpayer IDs, bank account details, and benefits claims information. The company began notifying affected individuals on June 30 with Kroll identity protection enrollment, though no ransomware group has claimed the attack and Kubota reported no operational disruption.

title: Trouble keeps finding Supermicro as strange server shipments attract police attention in Taiwan and Singapore
blurb: Supermicro is again under law-enforcement scrutiny after police in Taiwan and Singapore opened probes into irregular server shipments suspected of skirting export controls. The renewed attention adds vendor and supply-chain risk for enterprise buyers already wary of the company since its 2024 accounting troubles, raising the odds of shipment delays and tighter compliance diligence on any Supermicro hardware in the procurement pipeline.

title: Taiwan Detains Super Micro Workers in China Chip Smuggling Probe
blurb: Taiwanese prosecutors detained two Super Micro employees and raided the company's local offices this week over alleged shipments of Nvidia chips to China. The case extends US export-control enforcement into the supply chain of a major server vendor, raising compliance and sourcing risk for enterprises that buy Super Micro hardware and depend on tightly regulated Nvidia GPU allocations.

title: GitHub’s new tool helps prevent costly open-source license violations
blurb: GitHub released License Compliance in public preview for GitHub Advanced Security customers, adding automated scanning that flags open-source dependencies in pull requests whose licenses need review against an organization's policy. Enterprise Cloud teams with an active GHAS Code Security license can run it in Evaluate mode to annotate PRs without blocking merges, then tighten to approvals and package-specific exceptions as their license policy matures.

title: Nissan employee data exposed in Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day attacks
blurb: Nissan disclosed a data breach in which attackers exploited a zero-day in Oracle's PeopleSoft HR software to expose current and former employees' contact details, banking information, social security numbers, and tax data across the US, Canada, Mexico, and Brazil. The incident is part of a ShinyHunters campaign linked to Oracle PeopleSoft flaws that has hit more than 100 organizations, putting pressure on enterprises running the payroll and personnel suite to patch and audit access.

title: Cloudflare to block cynical search-and-scrape bots from ad-supported web pages
blurb: Cloudflare will let publishers block bots that scrape ad-supported pages for AI training while still allowing legitimate search crawlers, addressing the problem that dual-purpose crawlers force sites to choose between protecting content and staying in search results. The move gives publishers finer-grained control over their bot policies and pressures crawler operators like Google and OpenAI to separate their search and training traffic.

title: SAP, AI 조직 CEO 직속으로 재편…제품·엔지니어링 총괄 체계 개편
blurb: SAP restructured its product and engineering leadership for the second time this year, folding most of departing board member Muhammad Alam's portfolio directly under CEO Christian Klein and moving industry AI to COO Sebastian Steinhäuser. The move consolidates AI development at the top as SAP pushes from a SaaS-first to an AI-first vendor, and it will search externally, largely in the US, for a new product chief.

title: T-Mobile US also has Broadcom beef over VMware changes: report
blurb: T-Mobile joined the growing list of enterprises fighting Broadcom over post-acquisition VMware terms, winning a court injunction that forces Broadcom to keep supporting the roughly 303,000 CPU cores running the carrier's internal network after its pre-buyout perpetual-license deal lapsed. The dispute shows how Broadcom's shift to VMware Cloud Foundation subscriptions is voiding legacy support agreements and pushing even its largest customers toward migration and litigation.

title: The inside scoop on alerting changes in Kubernetes Monitoring
blurb: Grafana Cloud is consolidating on Grafana-managed alerts as its single alerting platform, which may break notifications after a Kubernetes Monitoring reinstall.

title: DRAM it! Cheap PCs being priced out of existence as memory cost bites
blurb: Surging DRAM prices are pushing entry-level PCs toward extinction, as memory costs climb fast enough that vendors can no longer hit the sub-budget price points that defined the low end of the market. For IT buyers, the squeeze raises fleet-refresh costs and narrows the cheap-endpoint options, pressuring hardware budgets already strained by the AI-driven demand that is diverting DRAM supply toward datacenters.

title: SAP Restricts Hiring, Travel to Fund ‘Significant’ AI Push
blurb: SAP said it will curb hiring and travel spending to redirect resources toward AI development as it defends its enterprise-software franchise against new entrants. The move signals that Europe's largest software vendor is prioritizing AI investment over headcount growth, a shift that could affect delivery timelines and support staffing for the customers running its ERP and cloud stack.

title: Amazon fined $2.25M for withholding evidence from fraud victims
blurb: The FTC fined Amazon $2.25M to settle charges that it violated Section 609(e) of the Fair Credit Reporting Act by blocking identity theft victims' access to fraudulent-transaction records, with customer service agents citing "privacy" reasons or missing the law's 30-day disclosure window. The order requires Amazon to supply lawfully requested records to victims and law enforcement within 30 days and to notify consumers who had requested records since April 2024 but never received them.

title: Citrix says it's back as a mainstream server virtualization player that won't send scary bills
blurb: Citrix relaunched itself as a mainstream server virtualization vendor, pitching predictable licensing against the pricing turmoil that followed Broadcom's VMware acquisition. The move targets enterprises hunting for a hypervisor alternative after VMware bundle changes drove up renewal costs, positioning Citrix to win migrations from IT shops wary of another round of unexpected bills.

title: Salesforce employees are confused about why the company is promoting a competitor inside Slack
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, an always-on AI agent that lives inside Slack channels, and Salesforce promoted it publicly even though it competes directly with Salesforce's own Slackbot and Agentforce. The overlap leaves Slack customers choosing between three agent layers in one workspace, and Salesforce has reason to tolerate it: it expects to spend $300M on Anthropic tokens this year and holds a roughly 1% stake in the lab.

title: US Federal Insurance Regulator Confirms Data Breach Via Oracle Flaw
blurb: The NAIC confirmed that attackers exploited a zero-day vulnerability in Oracle PeopleSoft to breach its environment and publish credit rating agency data used for insurer investment designations. The incident disrupted NAIC operations enough that some rating agencies paused data feeds and the association suspended assigning investment designations, though personal, payment, and state-system data were not compromised.

title: Enterprise Tech In, Shell Out (Progress Kemp LoadMaster Uninitialized Heap to Pre-Auth RCE CVE-2026-8037)
blurb: Progress disclosed CVE-2026-8037, a pre-authentication remote code execution flaw in Kemp LoadMaster, its edge load balancer and application delivery controller, exploitable by any unauthenticated attacker who can reach the API on GA builds v7.2.63.1 and older or LTSF v7.2.54.17 and older. Progress, the vendor behind the widely exploited MoveIt breaches, has patched the issue, and enterprises running LoadMaster at the network edge should update immediately or disable the API.

title: Preventing agent-generated infrastructure bloat through spec-driven governance
blurb: The author argues that autonomous engineering agents will industrialize infrastructure inefficiency at the same scale they deliver software, provisioning oversized clusters and bloated containers across every environment because the spec never told them otherwise. With more than a quarter of new code already AI-generated and Gartner projecting just 30% of large enterprises will embed sustainability in requirements by 2027, the fix is spec-driven policy checks in the pipeline, since post-deploy remediation no longer scales.

title: Google Loses EU Top Court Fight Over €4.1 Billion Android Fine
blurb: The EU's top court upheld a €4.1B ($4.7B) antitrust fine against Google, ruling that regulators correctly found the company abused Android's market power to entrench Search and Chrome on mobile devices. The decision closes an eight-year appeal and hardens the legal basis for Brussels to police default-app bundling, keeping pressure on Google's Android licensing terms with OEM partners across the bloc.

title: What the AI patch gap means for enterprise security
blurb: Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview flagged 1,596 verified vulnerabilities across hundreds of open-source projects over nine weeks, but security firm Tuskira found maintainers patched them at roughly one-sixteenth the discovery rate. The 16.5-to-one gap, paired with a 90.8% true-positive rate, means enterprises leaning on open-source dependencies now inherit a widening backlog of real, unpatched bugs that outpaces both maintainer fixes and public advisories.

title: New PC purchases see sharpest drop in nearly three years as memory and storage prices bite — shipments fall by 7%, analysts forecast 14% contraction that will hit budget laptops hard
blurb: U.S. PC shipments fell 7% year-over-year to 15.8 million units in Q1 2026, the sharpest quarterly drop since Q3 2023, as memory and storage shortages push component prices up. Omdia forecasts a 14.4% full-year contraction concentrated in sub-$500 laptops, with the education segment down a projected 28.8%, while Dell and Lenovo were the only vendors to grow.

title: New FBI Alert: Russian Intelligence Uses Signal Recovery Keys to Access Messages
blurb: The FBI and CISA updated their March 2026 advisory to warn that FSB-linked groups UNC5792 and UNC4221 have shifted their Signal phishing campaigns from stealing verification codes to eliciting victims' Backup Recovery Keys. Unlike a one-time code, a stolen recovery key unlocks the full historical message archive and stays valid even after the victim creates a new account on the same number, making account takeover persistent.

title: Mapping out your unknown: A threat hunter's guide to Salesforce
blurb: Datadog's Security Research team published the first part of a threat-hunting guide for Salesforce, documenting how recent campaigns abuse OAuth connected apps, compromised third-party integrations, and hijacked SSO sessions to enumerate and exfiltrate data. The post maps each attacker step to a MITRE ATT&CK tactic and provides audit-log queries tuned to Salesforce's logging tiers, from Event Log Files to Real-Time Event Monitoring. For enterprises with dozens of connected apps per Salesforce tenant, it is a practical baseline for detection coverage most teams currently lack.

title: Securing the code that decides who Entra trusts: a Function App and a Logic App, end to end
blurb: The author argues that Azure Function Apps and Logic Apps hosting Entra custom claims providers belong on the Control Plane alongside domain controllers, because a compromised extension can forge any claim in the token issuance pipeline and downstream apps will trust it as if Entra asserted it. The practical takeaway for IT: these resources inherit RBAC from far up the management-group tree, so they need Tier 0 isolation and guardrails, not the default placement most teams give production Function Apps.

title: Securing AI agents: When AI tools move from reading to acting
blurb: Microsoft Incident Response details an attack pattern that poisons Model Context Protocol tool metadata to hijack enterprise agents as they shift from reading content to executing actions like sending email or updating records. The post maps the technique to the new OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications and gives defenders a playbook for detection and containment, arguing that vulnerabilities carry a higher impact profile once a prompt injection can trigger an action rather than just bias an output.

title: What Meta, Oracle moves say about data center economics
blurb: Oracle's latest 10-K and Meta's reported plan to resell excess compute signal that hyperscalers are shifting from an AI capacity land grab to managing the financial risk of the buildout. Oracle's filing details how supply-chain delays, permitting limits, and third-party defaults could inflate the cost of commitments like its $300B Stargate deal with OpenAI. With the top four hyperscalers set to spend up to $725B on AI infrastructure this year, the pressure is now on return, not just scale.

title: 가트너 “에이전틱 AI에 SaaS 시장 재편…기존 업체는 위기, 서비스 기업은 기회”
blurb: Gartner forecasts that "agentic arbitrage" will reshape up to $234B in enterprise application spending through 2030, roughly 20% of projected SaaS outlays, as AI agents execute work across systems and bypass the user interfaces vendors monetize. The report argues this breaks the link between seat growth and revenue, threatening dashboard- and per-user pricing models. Gartner's read: incumbents must shift from interface value to outcome value, while service firms and agentic-platform startups capture the freed budget.

title: AWS launches Secret Cloud for Industry
blurb: AWS launched the Secret Cloud for Industry (ASCI), a cloud region cleared to Impact Level 6 for secret-classified workloads run by U.S. defense contractors in the National Industrial Security Program, with Northrop Grumman as its first customer. Alongside it, AWS opened a $1B credit program running through October 2030 to migrate intelligence agencies off on-premises systems, an offer the CIA has already confirmed it will use.

title: Oracle outlines all the ways it could lose the farm it bet on AI
blurb: In its latest SEC filing, Oracle enumerated the risks around its multibillion-dollar AI infrastructure buildout, from customer concentration and reliance on a handful of large AI contracts to the chance that data-center spending outpaces the demand it is betting on. The disclosure underscores how much of Oracle's cloud growth story now hinges on OCI capacity commitments converting to durable revenue, a bet that leaves little room if a marquee customer pulls back.

title: Understanding dynamic resource allocation in Kubernetes
blurb: Dynamic Resource Allocation reached GA in Kubernetes v1.35, and this walkthrough runs GPU scheduling on a live cluster of NVIDIA T10 and A5000 cards to show how DRA replaces the old device-plugin model for claiming hardware. NVIDIA's dra-driver-nvidia-gpu has moved into Kubernetes SIGs and dropped its Beta label, signaling the standard is stabilizing. The post covers labeling GPU nodes, installing the GPU Operator v26.3.1, and wiring up resource claims on Kubernetes v1.35.3.

title: Apache Flink 2.3.0 Release Announcement
blurb: The Apache Flink PMC released Flink 2.3.0, which adds SQL changelog operators (FROM_CHANGELOG and TO_CHANGELOG), a redesigned native S3 filesystem built on AWS SDK v2, and application-level lifecycle management across 15 implemented FLIPs. Adaptive partition selection and improved watermark alignment cut backpressure and speed backlog processing, while a reworked SinkUpsertMaterializer lifts performance for some SQL workloads in production deployments.

title: Introducing Package Proxy: Supply-Chain Safety Checks Without Client-Side Software
blurb: Thinkst released Package Proxy, a Cloudflare Workers-based proxy that runs inline supply-chain checks for npm, pip, uv, and cargo without installing any client-side software on developer machines. The tool blocks packages that fall inside the risky window for known attack patterns, such as recently published versions tied to surreptitious back-doors and low-install typo-squats, and deploys from its GitHub repo straight into Cloudflare.

title: The "Akrites" vulnerability-mitigation project launches
blurb: Akrites launched as a vulnerability-mitigation project that fixes flaws upstream and confidentially, measuring success by patch deployment rather than publication on the premise that public patches let adversaries reverse-engineer exploits before fixes land. The program keeps undisclosed flaws embargoed, routes fixes through each project's own maintainers, and commits to serve as maintainer of last resort for unmaintained but critical packages.

title: What happens when you run a CUDA kernel
blurb: A deep technical walkthrough traces a single vector-add CUDA kernel from `nvcc` compilation down through PTX, SASS, and warp scheduling on an RTX 4090, then back up to the result. The author shows that printing one correct answer involves tens of millions of CPU instructions, roughly 900 ioctls, and a memory-mapped doorbell register the driver rings to launch work on the GPU. It is a useful mental model for anyone debugging GPU utilization or CUDA driver overhead in production.

title: Preventing agent-generated infrastructure bloat through spec-driven governance
blurb: The author argues that AI agents industrialize infrastructure waste at the same scale they ship code, provisioning oversized GKE clusters, inflated pod requests, and bloated base images that run continuously for a service's lifetime. The fix is spec-driven governance: encode sustainability constraints as policy checks against Terraform and YAML inside the pipeline, blocking inefficient patterns pre-deploy rather than right-sizing after the fact, which becomes impractical once agentic pipelines reproduce the same waste across every environment.

title: Google Antigravity agents get full context with GitLab Orbit
blurb: GitLab released Orbit, a lifecycle context graph that Google Antigravity developers can install from the Antigravity MCP Store to give coding agents structured access to projects, pipelines, merge requests, vulnerabilities, and source across a GitLab instance. Orbit indexes the instance into a knowledge graph queried through two MCP tools, and GitLab's internal tests reported agents responding up to 11x faster, using 4.5x fewer tokens, and producing 45x fewer hallucinations.

title: T-Mobile moving tens of thousands of virtual machines off VMware amid lawsuit
blurb: T-Mobile sued Broadcom over dropped VMware perpetual-license support while migrating tens of thousands of VMs across 303,140 cores off the platform.

title: Announcing the Monetization Gateway: charge for any resource behind Cloudflare via x402
